L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump from a guyed antenna in medium-to-strong wind conditions. Strong crosswinds blew him into a guy wire under canopy, causing canopy collapse at 300 feet. The decision to jump in winds strong enough to drift a canopy into guy wires from a three-guy-wire antenna — which inherently restricts safe heading range — was clearly and specifically inappropriate, and this decision was the producing mechanism for the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Rigging error: pull-up cord left attached
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Frank left a pull-up cord in the closing loop of his container, which prevented the static line from extracting the canopy, resulting in a total malfunction. This is a memory/attention failure during the rigging process — he intended to complete the rigging correctly but failed to remove a tool that blocked container opening. The omission directly produced the total malfunction and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in gusty conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a short delay cliff jump in gusty mid-day conditions with crosswinds at the exit. The narrative explicitly identifies strong/medium winds and crosswinds as possible factors, and gusty mid-day conditions are clearly inappropriate for a slider-down cliff jump where an off-heading opening leaves minimal time and separation to avoid object strike. The decision to jump in these conditions was the producing mechanism: the gusty crosswinds caused the 180° off-heading opening that put the jumper into the wall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate static line attachment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Pauli used a loose single knot to connect the static line break cord to the parachute, while other jumpers used double knots. This inadequate knot broke prematurely during deployment, causing the static line to disconnect before it could extract the pilot chute from the bag. The jump became an unassisted freefall from a 170m antenna using a slow-opening system with insufficient altitude for inflation, resulting in impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to disconnect from gear in water
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After landing in the river and signaling he was all right, the jumper made no attempt to get out of his gear. His reserve canopy caught the swift current and pulled him under the surface, causing drowning. The failure to disconnect from the parachute system after a water landing was the direct mechanism that produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to correct off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a clean 180° off-heading opening, Carl was unable to turn the canopy away from the wall before striking it. The canopy's severe forward surge upon opening (due to unmodified brake settings lacking a deep brake setting) combined with the 180° off-heading resulted in the canopy flying him into the wall before he could execute a heading correction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped unmodified canopy without DBS
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Carl refused to allow modification of his canopy with a deep brake setting or modern tail pouch before the trip, despite a known severe surge characteristic on opening. The absence of a DBS meant the canopy had excessive forward speed upon inflation, directly producing the fatal wall strike when the 180° off-heading occurred. A properly tuned DBS would have reduced forward speed and provided additional time for heading correction, which is the explicit purpose of the DBS for object-proximate jumps.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jorgen exited head first, went into a spin, and lost control, impacting the cliff face at subterminal altitude near the exit. The head-first exit resulted in an unrecoverable spin, indicating a technique/execution error in the exit rather than a decision error. The fatal outcome was produced by the inability to recover a stable body position and deploy the parachute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper 'went off unstable' from a technically difficult waterfall. This unstable exit led to catching her feet in lines during deployment, producing a 180 off-heading opening and fatal object strike. The execution failure on exit was the producing mechanism for the chain of events that caused the fatality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper launched head down and lost control, then deployed while unstable. This execution failure — an unstable exit leading to unstable deployment — resulted in an off-heading opening and fatal object strike under canopy. Despite 2,700 skydives, this was his first BASE jump, and the physical execution of the exit was insufficient for the cliff environment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient information on error type
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a slow opening and 180-degree off-heading, resulting in landing in rapids and drowning. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., the choice to use skydiving gear with a sail slider) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., poor body position causing the slow opening and off-heading, or failure to correct heading). Multiple possible factors exist but the narrative lacks detail to discriminate between them.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydiving gear for BASE jump
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used skydiving equipment rather than dedicated BASE equipment for a 500-foot antenna jump. Skydiving gear has smaller pilot chutes and deployment systems designed for terminal velocity freefall from altitude, not the low-airspeed, low-altitude conditions of a slider-down BASE jump from 500 feet. This equipment selection decision resulted in a total malfunction (impact with nothing out), as the skydiving pilot chute was inadequate to extract and deploy the canopy in the available time and airspeed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Static line misrigging
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The static line parted prior to extracting the canopy, resulting in a total malfunction. This was Mike's first solo pack job, and the cause of death is listed as malfunction/misrigging, indicating a rigging execution error in the static line setup that caused it to fail before canopy extraction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a history of unstable exits (head-down launch on his previous jump) and the narrative lists unstable exit as a possible factor. The canopy opened with line twists and an off-heading orientation resulting in object strike, indicating poor body position at deployment produced the fatal conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate equipment selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used a skydiving rig with a skydiving canopy (Pegasus) configured slider-down for a 6-second delay from a cliff. Skydiving equipment is not designed for BASE applications - skydiving canopies have poor heading performance compared to BASE canopies, and skydiving containers are not optimized for the deployment dynamics of BASE jumping. Using skydiving gear for a slider-down cliff jump with only one prior BASE jump represents a clearly inappropriate equipment decision that was a producing mechanism for the fatal off-heading opening and object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydiving deployment system for BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to use a skydiving pilot chute and skydiving bridle for a BASE jump despite being advised by Bridge Day Jumpmasters that this was not the appropriate setup. The skydiving PC failed to inflate (appearing inverted/mesh-side-out in video), which is consistent with the known inadequacy of skydiving PCs for BASE applications — they are too small and lack the drag characteristics needed at sub-terminal airspeeds. This equipment decision directly produced the total malfunction that led to impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative reports medium to strong winds at the time of the jump. The jumper died by striking a seawall on final approach under canopy, indicating wind conditions prevented a safe landing. Jumping in strong winds is a clear departure from accepted practice, as the domain reference states few objects are good in winds over 10 mph and no objects are good over 20 mph. The strong winds were the producing mechanism that made a safe landing approach impossible, resulting in the fatal seawall strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper over-delayed freefall and deployed the pilot chute too late, resulting in impact with the talus at line stretch. The jumper failed to deploy at the correct altitude, indicating an execution/attention failure in monitoring altitude or timing rather than a deliberate decision to pull at an inadequate altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Skydive gear for BASE jump
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jean-Marc used a skydiving reserve canopy (Vega 180) packed in a deployment bag for a BASE jump from a cliff (Angel Falls). Using skydive gear for BASE jumping is a clearly inappropriate equipment decision: skydiving reserve canopies lack the heading performance characteristics of BASE-specific canopies, and deployment bag configurations at sub-terminal or low airspeeds yield poor heading performance. The cause of death is explicitly attributed to 'Skydive Gear Used,' and the resulting 180 off-heading with line twists led to wall strike and fatal injuries.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Improper PC hold technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David misunderstood advice about hand-held PC technique and exposed several inches of mesh in addition to the top of the pilot chute. This caused the PC to partially inflate before release, which tipped him backwards and torqued him during opening, producing multiple line twists he could not clear before striking the wall. The narrative explicitly states he misunderstood the instruction — he intended to hold the PC correctly for faster inflation but executed the technique incorrectly by exposing too much mesh.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Off-heading with line twists cause unclear
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening with line twists from a PCA deployment on a building, resulting in object strike. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the off-heading and line twists resulted from a decision error (e.g., inadequate planning) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., poor body position at deployment, packing error). The root cause of the off-heading opening cannot be determined from the available information.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydiving container for BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to use a skydiving rig (Wonderhog) with a curved pin closure system for a BASE jump instead of a purpose-built BASE container. The skydiving container's curved pin closure was susceptible to pin lock under tension, and this equipment choice was the producing mechanism for the fatal pilot chute in tow. The narrative explicitly notes both jumpers had discussed purchasing proper BASE containers but declined due to cost, establishing this was a conscious equipment decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Head-down exit causing 180 off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Darren exited very head-down, causing the canopy to deploy between his legs and open 180 degrees off-heading, surging into the building. He had been advised multiple times to correct his exits as his previous three jumps were all head-down, indicating a persistent technique error in exit body position. The head-down exit directly produced the off-heading opening and fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature PC deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jonathan's pilot chute inadvertently deployed from the BOC pouch during freefall tracking, causing a premature opening. He had never used this BOC style before, and the pouch was reportedly too large for the pilot chute, allowing fabric to catch air. The premature deployment was an execution failure - the PC was not adequately secured, and his unfamiliarity with the system contributed to improper stowage (pulling fabric out of the pouch before exit).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate off-heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading opening and failed to adequately respond with heading correction, resulting in object strike under canopy. The narrative explicitly states he 'failed to adequately respond' to the off-heading, indicating a performance/execution failure in the critical task of heading correction after opening.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient forward separation on exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The possible factors identify 'not enough forward separation on exit' as a contributing factor. Insufficient push or exit technique resulted in inadequate object separation, reducing the time and distance available to survive the subsequent off-heading opening. This is a technique/execution error in performing the exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of altitude awareness
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper held a head-high attitude for too long during freefall from El Capitan, lost altitude awareness, and backed into the wall in her 15th second of freefall. The head-high body position and failure to monitor altitude and proximity to the cliff constituted a performance/execution error that directly produced the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydive gear for BASE jump
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used skydiving equipment for a BASE jump from an antenna. Skydiving gear is designed for terminal-velocity deployments with thousands of feet of altitude margin, not for the sub-terminal airspeeds and minimal altitude of BASE jumping. The slider-up skydiving canopy sniveled (prolonged opening sequence) at sub-terminal airspeed, consuming all available altitude and resulting in impact. This was a fundamental equipment selection decision error that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted BASE with no training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had no apparent BASE training, no BASE experience, and held only an expired USPA A license (the lowest skydiving certification). Attempting a BASE jump from an antenna without a First Jump Course, mentor guidance, or any understanding of BASE-specific equipment, packing, and deployment requirements represents a decision that was clearly and specifically inadequate. This decision directly enabled the use of inappropriate equipment and configuration.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate equipment and technique for object
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used an unmodified square canopy with no deep brake settings for a sheer cliff that was best suited for freefall delay and a round canopy. A square canopy without DBS produces significant forward speed on opening, and on a sheer wall this directly creates the pendulum/object strike failure mode. The equipment and technique selection was clearly inappropriate for the object's characteristics.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Stowed PC on static line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided at the last moment to stow the pilot chute in the BOC pouch instead of leaving it out as required for a static line deployment. This decision directly caused the malfunction: when the static line became taut, it applied force at a right angle to the stowed PC, causing the break cord to part prematurely before extracting the PC and bridle, resulting in a total malfunction. The domain reference explicitly states that the PC must never be stowed during a static line jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Canopy control error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Don flew himself into a corner after the bridge jump and panic-hooked into the canyon wall, resulting in fatal head trauma. The fatal outcome was produced by inadequate canopy piloting execution in the tight landing area — specifically a panic hook turn into the canyon wall rather than a controlled approach.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation during freefall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Sébastien was tracking on his back to film the second jumper. Instead of prioritizing forward separation from the cliff, both jumpers focused on closing the gap between them for video purposes. This resulted in inadequate separation from the wall, leaving them approximately 2 seconds from impact when it was time to separate and deploy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment due to task saturation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: By the time Sébastien recognized the insufficient separation and attempted to deploy, he was too close to the wall. He pitched immediately upon realizing the situation but impacted the wall during deployment, indicating the deployment was initiated too late to achieve a survivable outcome. The filming task degraded his monitoring of separation and altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with late recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had an unstable launch and took 12 seconds to recover stability before deploying the pilot chute, with impact occurring at 12.7 seconds. The execution failure was the unstable exit combined with the failure to deploy the parachute despite instability — the jumper prioritized regaining stability over deploying, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to open. Regardless of the exit quality, deploying earlier (even from an unstable position) was the critical execution failure that produced the fatal low pull.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to execute evasive maneuvers
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to apply immediate heading correction or evasive maneuvers after canopy opening near the cliff face. The original eyewitness account states he had ample room and time to employ commonly practiced evasive maneuvers but allowed his canopy to fly into the cliff. Even accounting for the update suggesting limited time due to the underhung wall geometry, the jumper had been repeatedly advised to be more responsive during the canopy opening sequence, indicating a pattern of inadequate execution of post-opening canopy control skills.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate brake setting on non-BASE canopy
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was repeatedly advised to apply deeper brake settings to his non-BASE specific canopy to avoid its excessive surge and drive, but did not do so. Using a canopy with excessive forward surge without appropriate deep brake settings at a cliff where the wall becomes underhung and openings occur directly beside the wall was a decision that directly contributed to the canopy flying into the cliff rather than flying slowly enough to allow heading correction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Used skydiving gear for BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used an unmodified skydiving rig for a BASE jump. A skydiving collapsible pilot chute with bungee retraction is designed for terminal airspeeds and is known to be ineffective at the sub-terminal airspeeds of a BASE jump from a 364-foot antenna. This equipment selection decision directly produced the total malfunction that caused the fatality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper delayed too long before extracting the pilot chute from the BOC, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to fully deploy. The possible factors note 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late' and the cause of death is listed as 'low pull,' indicating the jumper failed to deploy at the correct altitude despite presumably intending to do so.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flight into obscured conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper jumped into a fog bank (low level clouds), which eliminated visual references needed for canopy navigation near a cliff. Flying into conditions with no visibility near terrain is a clearly inappropriate decision, as it removed the ability to maintain object separation. The cause of death was object strike under canopy after reportedly losing bearings in the fog.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had an unstable head-down launch from the cliff. This unstable exit compromised his ability to establish an effective track away from the wall, resulting in insufficient object separation. He struck the wall at eight seconds, leading to canopy deflation and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff on exit, as indicated by the possible factors noting clipped ledge after exiting and not enough forward separation on exit. This insufficient separation, combined with late pilot chute extraction, resulted in the canopy opening with the jumper too close to the wall, leading to fatal wall impact with no time or distance for heading correction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a normal delay, the jumper instinctively reached for the BOC position she was accustomed to rather than the leg-mounted pilot chute pouch on the system she was actually jumping. She failed to locate the pilot chute handle and ran out of time before correcting, resulting in a no-pull impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in clearly adverse conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's companions explicitly warned him that it was too dark, the wind was wrong, and the water was too high, yet he elected to jump. This was a conscious decision to proceed despite multiple clearly identified hazards (night, adverse wind, high water) that collectively made the jump inappropriate. The decision to jump in these conditions — particularly the high, moving water that directly caused his drowning — was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate equipment selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Trevor used a skydiving container (Racer) with a skydiving-style deployment system: a 30-inch F-111 pilot chute, a 4-foot bridle, a deployment bag, and a sail slider. This equipment was fundamentally inappropriate for BASE jumping. The 30-inch F-111 PC was grossly undersized for a slider-up BASE deployment, the 4-foot bridle was far too short to clear the burble (BASE standard is 9 feet), and the sail slider would have severely degraded heading performance and produced dangerously slow openings. The combination of these equipment choices was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome — the canopy was found at line stretch with the slider still at the top of the lines, indicating the system never completed deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment due to altitude awareness loss
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Andrea over-delayed while flying in a back-to-earth position filming two other jumpers, failing to deploy his pilot chute at a survivable altitude. He impacted the talus as his canopy was still opening, indicating the deployment was initiated too late. The back-to-earth position is a known cause of loss of altitude awareness, and the filming task divided his attention from monitoring altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor tracking and deployment technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Video evidence shows Terry having trouble with directional control and tracking during freefall. He deployed with a 180° off-heading, resulting in his canopy flying him directly into the cliff wall. With only 8 BASE jumps, his sub-terminal tracking and body position at deployment were insufficient to achieve adequate object separation and on-heading opening, constituting a performance/skill-based error in execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit no recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper launched unstable and remained unstable throughout freefall until impact. This is a performance/execution failure: the jumper failed to recover a stable body position and failed to deploy the parachute despite having terminal freefall time available (slider-up cliff, impact at terminal velocity). The inability to stabilize and/or deploy constitutes a skill-based execution error that directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in clearly unsuitable weather
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly describes conditions as 'pretty bad' with rain, strong and gusty winds, and crosswinds from the right. The group acknowledged conditions were 'better, but not good' and that everyone would have left if anyone had suggested it. The jumper chose to jump in conditions that were clearly inadequate for safe cliff jumping - strong gusty crosswinds, rain, and turbulence near the wall. These conditions directly produced the fatal outcome: the crosswind pushed the jumper into the wall corner despite the group's plan to fly in the wind shadow.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Deployed too high above wind shadow
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The group agreed on a plan to deploy low enough to remain in the wind shadow of the wall but high enough to handle heading problems. Jorge deployed his canopy higher than all the other jumpers, placing himself above the protective wind shadow and into the turbulent crosswind zone near the wall. This decision directly resulted in his canopy being exposed to the strong crosswinds and turbulence, causing him to fly parallel to the wall until impacting the corner.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping with no training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had no previous BASE jumps, no training, no mentor, and no experienced jumper present. He purchased BASE gear and attempted a BASE jump from a sub-optimal exit point with zero preparation. This represents a clear and specific decision error — attempting a BASE jump without any of the foundational skills, training, or mentorship required, which directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper pivoted off the launch, went on his back, and struck the cliff in freefall. The execution of the exit was fatally flawed — he failed to achieve any stable body position or forward separation from the cliff, directly resulting in object strike at sub-terminal altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Rigging omission during conversion
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper converted from slider-down to slider-up configuration but neglected to route the steering lines back through the slider grommets. This omission is a memory/procedural execution failure — he knew the correct procedure (having packed slider-up configurations before) but failed to complete a necessary step during the last-minute conversion. The misrouted steering lines caused the canopy to open in a turn, resulting in object strike before full inflation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Back-to-earth exit at cliff
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Thierry chose to exit on his back from a cliff with only ~6 seconds of pure vertical. A back-to-earth exit is an aerobatic maneuver that inherently costs time and altitude to recover to a belly-to-earth orientation, and the domain reference explicitly identifies loss of altitude awareness and delayed deployment as known failure modes of back-to-earth exits. At a cliff requiring mandatory tracking for separation, this decision consumed the altitude and time needed for recovery, tracking, and deployment. The execution proceeded as intended (he exited on his back as planned), but the decision itself was the producing mechanism — choosing an aerobatic exit from a cliff with limited vertical relief and insufficient margin for the recovery time required.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate object separation and delayed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the back-to-earth exit, Thierry took too long to recover to a belly-to-earth position facing away from the wall. He failed to achieve adequate forward separation through tracking before needing to deploy. His attempted quarter-turn and deployment came too late, resulting in ground impact before canopy deployment was complete. The execution of the recovery and tracking was insufficient for the altitude available.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had an unstable short delay from a slider-up cliff object, resulting in insufficient forward separation. With only 3 BASE jumps total (second or third jump), she lacked the sub-terminal tracking skill and exit technique needed to achieve adequate object separation from a slider-up cliff, leading to a wall strike under canopy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit from wet ramp
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper slipped on the wet launch ramp, resulting in loss of control during freefall. He deployed on his back with a partially inflated canopy and struck the tree-filled outcropping. The execution failure on exit — slipping — produced the unstable freefall, back-to-earth deployment, and fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate gear selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used modified skydiving gear including a PO-9 skydiving canopy known in the Russian BASE community as unsuitable for BASE jumping. Using a skydiving canopy and modified skydiving equipment for a slider-down BASE jump from a building is a fundamentally inappropriate equipment decision. The gear selection itself was the producing mechanism: a skydiving canopy lacks the design features (reinforcement, heading performance, appropriate opening characteristics, deep brake settings) required for low-altitude fixed-object deployments, and this inadequacy directly produced the fatal outcome regardless of execution quality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper went head down on the launch and tumbled, failing to achieve a stable exit position. This unstable exit resulted in the jumper striking the wall before any effective deployment could occur, with the canopy only partially deploying from the force of wall impact. The execution of the exit was the direct producing mechanism of the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / low pull
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Lee continued filming on his back for approximately 3 seconds beyond the second jumper's 8-second deployment, then took an additional second to transition to face-to-earth before disappearing into fog. He deployed too late to achieve a fully inflated canopy before ground impact. The narrative indicates he intended a 10-second delay but the total elapsed time before deployment initiation exceeded what the available altitude could support, constituting a failure in execution — specifically an attention failure in monitoring altitude/time while focused on filming.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment from stow
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a pilot chute in tow, meaning the PC failed to extract the canopy from the container. He was going stowed for the very first time, using a 38-inch PC with a 6'9" bridle on a 1.5-second delay from a span. The PC opened only 0.4 seconds before impact, indicating it remained entangled or ineffective throughout the brief freefall. The execution of the stowed PC deployment was inadequate - whether through weak throw, late deployment, or improper technique - resulting in the PC failing to catch clean air and extract the canopy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Lori launched and lost stability on her first cliff jump. She was unable to recover a stable body position or achieve adequate separation from the cliff, resulting in impact with the cliff wall in freefall at approximately 11 seconds. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating she never deployed her parachute, likely due to loss of altitude awareness and/or proximity to the wall resulting from the sustained instability.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and premature deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Nik launched head-down and deployed before achieving complete stability. This unstable body position at deployment directly contributed to the off-heading opening (canopy opened facing the cliff), which produced the fatal object strike. The execution of the exit and the timing of deployment were insufficient — he intended a 9-10 second delay but deployed while still unstable, indicating a performance failure in maintaining stability and/or deploying at the appropriate time.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient tracking separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: With most previous BASE jumps being low objects, Nik was actively working on tracking skills but did not achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff during freefall. The insufficient object separation meant that when the canopy opened off-heading (facing the cliff), there was not enough distance to complete the heading correction before striking the ledge. This is an execution/technique error in sub-terminal tracking performance.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Wolfgang extracted his pilot chute too late after attempting to film his jump partner's opening. The canopy lines were completely out of the tail pocket but the canopy only opened after impact, indicating deployment was initiated at an altitude insufficient for completion. The filming task degraded his tracking and delayed his pull, resulting in a fatal low deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited from a sketchy launch point and never gained control from exit through impact. With only 16 BASE jumps at a slider-up cliff requiring sub-terminal tracking, the jumper failed to execute a stable exit and was unable to recover a flyable body position, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment during wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Kirill failed to deploy his parachute at a survivable altitude during his wingsuit BASE flight. The update clarifies the canopy was fully open but he 'hooked ground' immediately after opening, indicating deployment occurred too late for the canopy to arrest his descent before terrain contact. With only 2 wingsuit BASE jumps and 20 wingsuit skydives, his altitude awareness and deployment timing execution were insufficient for the demands of wingsuit BASE flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose unachievable exit/line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Rob selected an exit point ('RT Hjjrner') with only a 5-second rock drop and a ledge that multiple experienced wingsuit pilots, including his jumping partner, assessed as not achievable. Despite repeated warnings that the jump could not be made, he committed to the exit including a reverse gainer. The decision to jump this specific site was the producing mechanism: the object's geometry did not provide sufficient clearance for a wingsuit flight path, resulting in impact with the talus ledge at subterminal speed with no possibility of parachute deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Incomplete bridle attachment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Bill passed the bridle through the canopy attachment ring but failed to complete the lark's head knot that would secure the connection. The narrative speculates he was distracted during rigging and did not finish the procedure, resulting in the bridle not being connected to the canopy. This rigging execution error directly produced a total malfunction — the pilot chute and bridle extracted cleanly but the canopy remained in the container, causing closed-container impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate delay for conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used a 5-6 second delay at a site where locals recommend at least 8-9 seconds for adequate object separation. Combined with medium to strong winds and crosswinds at exit, this short delay left the jumper too close to the wall at opening, directly producing the conditions for the fatal object strike when the 180° off-heading occurred. The decision to use an insufficient delay in strong/crosswind conditions was the producing mechanism: more delay would have provided the tracking time and separation needed to survive the off-heading.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew line requiring terrain clearance without margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flying line that required clearing a ledge, at a site known by only a very few jumpers. She was unable to outfly the ledge with her wingsuit, resulting in terrain impact in terminal freefall. The decision to fly a line that committed her to clearing specific terrain features without adequate margin was the producing mechanism — she did not attempt to pull, indicating she was in a no-pull zone when the terrain strike became inevitable. This represents a flight planning decision that was clearly inadequate for the terrain, independent of execution quality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of non-deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper was performing aerials and either over-delayed or had a pilot chute/bridle hang-up, with the PC deploying but the canopy not inflating prior to impact. The narrative explicitly presents two competing explanations (over-delay vs. PC/bridle entanglement) without resolving which occurred. There is insufficient information to determine whether this was a decision error (choosing excessive delay for aerials) or a performance/skill-based error (execution failure during aerials leading to late deployment or entanglement).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped with entangled PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper dropped the hand-held pilot chute, and it became entangled with him during pickup. He then jumped anyway with the entangled PC, resulting in a PC in tow / bridle entanglement and total malfunction. The decision to jump with a compromised deployment system — regardless of whether he was fully aware of the extent of the entanglement — was the producing mechanism for the fatality. Jumping with a known-disturbed PC configuration under pressure from law enforcement was a clearly inappropriate decision; aborting and accepting arrest was the obvious alternative.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Dropped hand-held PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper dropped the hand-held pilot chute while rushing to the exit point under pressure from highway patrol. This execution error created the entangled PC condition that led to the fatal total malfunction, though the subsequent decision to jump with the compromised PC was the primary producing mechanism.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late or failed deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative indicates a deployment problem—either the jumper initiated the deployment sequence too late or experienced pilot chute hesitation, resulting in a low pull and ground impact. There is insufficient detail to determine whether this was a decision error (consciously choosing to delay deployment) or a performance/skill-based error (failing to execute a timely pull or poor PC deployment technique).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to correct off-heading opening
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a 180-degree off-heading opening approximately 200 meters off the ground while facing the cliff. He struck the cliff approximately 3 seconds after opening without successfully correcting the heading. The narrative indicates this was his first off-heading opening, suggesting he lacked the practiced muscle memory for instant heading correction (rear riser or brake input) needed to turn away from the wall within the available time window.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment after locating PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: All witnesses agree that the jumper reached for and located the pilot chute at a reasonable altitude but then kept his hand there and continued in freefall without deploying. He finally deployed at approximately 30 feet above ground, far too late for the canopy to inflate. This represents a failure in execution — the jumper found the handle but failed to extract and deploy the PC in a timely manner, likely due to an attention/perception failure (misreading altitude due to unfamiliar groundrush characteristics in wingsuit flight). The intended action was correct but the execution was fatally delayed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Over-rotated aerial exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jason performed a single front somersault that over-rotated, which delayed the start of his track. This execution error directly reduced the time and distance available for tracking away from the wall, resulting in insufficient separation to clear the underhung section before impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted his pilot chute too late, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to fully open before ground impact. The narrative states he was observed with only a partially open canopy at impact, and the possible factors field identifies 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late.' The planned deployment sequence (reach for PC, delay one second while partner deploys) was executed too late in the freefall, constituting a performance error in altitude awareness and deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit from aerials
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed aerials and subsequently encountered stability problems. This instability led to a late deployment, which combined with an off-heading opening resulted in insufficient separation from the wall and a fatal object strike under canopy. The execution of the aerials produced the instability that cascaded into the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped from inadequate altitude
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump from the second level of the Eiffel Tower at 370 feet AGL with only 170 feet of clearance to the first level at 200 feet AGL. With a slider-down BOC deployment from a structure where the jump path passes directly over a protruding lower platform only 170 feet below, the decision to jump from this level created an inadequate margin for canopy deployment and object clearance. The jumper impacted the first level of the tower, indicating the 170-foot window was insufficient for the deployment sequence and any deviation in trajectory.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with the pilot chute still stowed in the leg pouch. He was unable to locate or extract the pilot chute, resulting in a total malfunction (no deployment). This is an execution failure — the jumper intended to deploy but failed to accomplish the physical act of extracting and deploying the PC.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew unfamiliar wingsuit in adverse conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew a Birdman S3 wingsuit for the first time in BASE, having only 11 prior wingsuit BASE jumps (all on a different, smaller suit — the BM GTI), in medium-to-strong crosswind conditions. Accepted practice requires 40-60 skydives in a specific suit before BASE jumping it, and the jumper's total wingsuit BASE experience (11 jumps) was well below the 100-jump minimum recommended before first wingsuit BASE. Flying an unfamiliar higher-performance suit in wind created the instability that prevented deployment, making this decision the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment due to instability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced instability at deployment time while wearing a tracking suit, tumbled, and then attempted to regain stability before deploying the pilot chute. This delay in extracting the PC—prioritizing stability recovery over immediate deployment at an altitude where margins were already thin—resulted in impact at approximately line stretch. The execution failure was in not deploying promptly despite the low altitude, and in the technique breakdown that caused the instability and tumble during the deployment sequence.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to remove packing aid
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to remove the pull-up cord used as a packing aid from the pilot chute prior to jumping. This memory/attention failure during the packing and pre-jump process resulted in the pilot chute being unable to extract the canopy, causing a total malfunction and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and poor body position
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper over-rotated head down before deploying the pilot chute, resulting in an unstable body position at deployment. This unstable deployment produced a 180° off-heading opening facing the cliff. Despite attempting riser corrections, the jumper was unable to clear the wall in time, resulting in fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of wall impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall and was found hung up on the wall with his canopy out at approximately 300 meters from the bottom. He was known for flying close to the wall and was wearing a tracking suit on a slider-up cliff jump. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the fatal outcome resulted from a specific decision error (e.g., choosing a line too close to terrain) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., loss of control, failed execution of proximity flight, delayed deployment). The possible factors listed include loss of speed-glide and proximity flying, but the mechanism connecting these to the outcome is not clearly established.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Shannon reached for her stowed pilot chute and either missed it or lost her grip during extraction from the BOC. Witnesses report her last attempt resulted in the pilot chute ending up on her back and hesitating, leading to a total malfunction and impact with nothing out. The intended action (deploying the PC) was correct, but the physical execution failed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Turned wrong direction wrapping bridle
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After hooking up his static line pilot chute and turning to face away from the tower, Mario turned the wrong way, wrapping the bridle around his lower body. This execution error — turning in the incorrect direction — directly produced the bridle entanglement that prevented normal canopy deployment and caused the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Over-rotation of front loop
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a front loop aerial but over-rotated, going fully head-down for almost the entire freefall while trying to correct. This execution failure consumed all available altitude and directly resulted in the fatal low deployment. The jumper intended to complete the aerial successfully but failed in the physical execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment into clean air
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Video shows Brian deployed his pilot chute into his chest at the 5-second mark rather than into clean airflow to the side. The PC became trapped against his body for approximately 2 seconds, and when it finally released at the 7-second mark, the canopy had insufficient time to fully deploy before impact at 8.5 seconds. The jumper intended to deploy but failed in the execution of throwing the PC into clean air, which is the fundamental technique requirement for PC deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of total malfunction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a total malfunction with the container remaining closed and the bridle tightly wound around his ankle. Witnesses reported the pilot chute appeared too small (suggesting a possible retained packing aid), but post-accident examination found the pilot chute free of any packing aids. The bridle-ankle entanglement could have resulted from a deployment technique error (e.g., the slightly head-low body position at pitch contributing to the bridle wrapping around the ankle) or from a rigging/packing error, but the narrative does not provide sufficient detail to distinguish whether this was a decision error or a performance/skill-based error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late tracking initiation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper launched back-to-earth and did not initiate tracking until very close to the outcropping. The narrative indicates he failed to begin tracking in time to achieve adequate separation from the wall, which is a performance/execution failure in the timing and initiation of a known required action. By the time he recognized the problem and deployed, he had insufficient separation and struck the cliff during canopy opening.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment due to delayed recovery from back-to-earth position
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Edgar exited on his back to film the other jumper and then attempted to turn face-to-earth while very unstable, resulting in a late and likely poorly executed deployment. The witness observed the unstable transition, and the delayed extraction of the pilot chute left insufficient altitude and separation for the canopy to fly clear of the wall. The domain reference explicitly identifies back-to-earth exits as having 'loss of altitude awareness' as a known serious failure mode, and deploying from unstable/non-belly-to-earth positions risks poor openings and entanglements.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Back-to-earth filming exit from cliff
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Edgar chose to exit a slider-up cliff on his back to film the other jumper. This decision was clearly and specifically inappropriate: the domain reference identifies back-to-earth exits as inherently dangerous due to loss of altitude awareness, and aerials from any object exponentially increase danger. Combining a back-to-earth exit with the task of filming another jumper at a cliff requiring object separation created a situation where even competent execution was unlikely to produce adequate separation, as the jumper sacrificed tracking time and object-avoidance capability for camera work. The narrative notes this was the fifth fatality involving flying back-to-earth with a camera.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in insufficient altitude for the canopy to fully deploy before ground impact. Associates confirmed this was a low pull. The jumper had prior experience on this object and conditions were good, indicating the intended action (deploying the PC) was correct but execution was fatally delayed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient information on cause
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper opened his canopy but was unable to reach the planned dry landing zone and landed in cold water approximately 100 feet from shore, ultimately drowning from hypothermia. The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the failure to reach the LZ resulted from a decision error (e.g., inadequate wind/conditions assessment, poor jump planning) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., poor canopy piloting, delayed heading correction after an off-heading opening). Multiple possible factors exist — wind conditions (light and variable with snow), possible off-heading opening, unfamiliar new container, canopy performance — but the narrative does not establish which was the producing mechanism.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in extreme winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump in 32-39 mph winds described as 'very strong and extremely turbulent' — conditions so severe that all local experienced jumpers considered it impossible anyone would jump. The domain reference states few objects are good above 10 mph and no objects are good above 20 mph; these winds were roughly double that threshold, representing a clear and specific departure from accepted practice that directly produced the fatal conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Evidence indicates the bridle was found wrapped around the jumper's hand, and impact occurred with a closed container but fully deployed PC. This indicates the jumper failed to cleanly release the pilot chute into airflow — holding the PC too long or failing to execute a clean throw in the extreme wind conditions, resulting in a wind-induced bridle entanglement and total malfunction (PC in tow).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor wingsuit exit and flight execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jimmy exited head high and remained in a near-stall configuration for 7 seconds, failing to achieve efficient flight promptly. This poor exit technique and slow-speed flight consumed critical altitude during the start arc, placing him too low to recover. The domain reference explicitly identifies exiting too head-high and flying too slowly/near-stall as common wingsuit execution errors that degrade start performance and kill available lift.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued flight at unsurvivable altitude
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the poor start consumed his altitude margin, Jimmy flew for approximately 22 seconds just above the snow in the couloir at an altitude too low to deploy safely, rather than deploying his parachute when he still had usable altitude. He matched his glide path to the terrain in a no-pull zone for 20 seconds before finally deploying, at which point impact was immediate. The decision to continue flying at an altitude where deployment could not produce a survivable outcome, rather than deploying earlier when altitude may still have permitted it, was the proximate mechanism of the fatal ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in marginal wind conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The group waited over 3 hours due to very strong headwinds, and when they jumped the winds were described as only 'marginally ok' at 4-5 mph. Given the jumper's limited cliff experience (only 3 cliff jumps, with remaining 62 jumps from 60-80 meter antennas), jumping a slider-down cliff in marginal wind conditions after prolonged strong winds represented a clearly inappropriate decision. The crosswinds at exit contributed to the fatal off-heading and object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient exit separation and late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff and extracted the pilot chute too late (1-2 second delay with insufficient push). With only 3 prior cliff jumps and 62 jumps from short antennas, the jumper lacked the cliff-specific skills to execute the exit and deployment with sufficient object separation. The result was an object strike under canopy only 230 meters below the exit, with the jumper found hung up with toggles in hands, indicating the canopy opened but with insufficient separation to avoid the cliff face.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of control during turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: While executing a sharp right turn to exit the 3rd valley during proximity wingsuit flight, the jumper's right arm wing folded under and he lost stability. Despite attempting to correct, he remained unstable and impacted terrain approximately 2 seconds later. The execution of the turn itself was the producing mechanism — the hard bank caused the wing to collapse, leading to unrecoverable instability and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff during freefall, resulting in being too close to the wall at deployment time. The narrative states he 'was too close to the wall to continue freefall' and had to pitch early. This insufficient separation — whether from a poor exit, inadequate tracking, or both — meant that when the canopy opened with a 180° off-heading, he had no margin to avoid object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Off-heading not corrected
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper opened with a 180° off-heading facing the wall and was unable to correct heading before striking the wall. While the lack of separation was the primary producing mechanism, the inability to correct the off-heading in the available time contributed directly to the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate anchor knot
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Marcelo tied a rope to the tower as a static line anchor, but the knot came undone upon loading at exit. This execution failure in securing the anchor meant the static line system could not extract and deploy the canopy, resulting in a total malfunction and fatal impact. The static line concept was appropriate for the object; the failure was in the physical execution of tying a secure knot.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Improper PC deployment technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative and post-accident gear inspection indicate that Oleg extracted the pilot chute from the BOC but held onto it before throwing, allowing the bridle to stream into the airflow and form a loop/knot around the PC. This resulted in a PC/bridle entanglement rendering the PC unable to extract the canopy (total malfunction - container remained closed until impact). The domain reference explicitly identifies holding the PC too long after extraction as a cause of PC/bridle entanglement, and states the correct technique is to immediately throw the PC into clean air. This was an execution error in deployment technique rather than a decision error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Cutaway after tree landing
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After experiencing a 180° off-heading and landing in a tall tree, the jumper cut away his canopy and fell approximately 40 feet to the ground. Cutting away from a tree-entangled canopy that is supporting the jumper is a clearly inappropriate decision, as the canopy was the only thing preventing a fatal fall. Whether motivated by fear of being caught or other factors, the decision to release the canopy while suspended at height was the producing mechanism for the fatal injuries.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of PC in tow
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a pilot chute in tow malfunction and was unable to extract the canopy despite actively attempting to clear it. The canopy was found still neatly folded beside him with closing loops intact, suggesting pin extraction occurred but the deployment chain failed. The exact cause of the PC in tow could not be determined from gear inspection; speculation centers on the wingsuit shrivel flap on the bridle potentially snagging during extraction due to how the bridle was stowed, but this remains unconfirmed. There is insufficient information to determine whether this was a decision error (e.g., improper bridle stowage choice) or a performance/skill-based error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment due to loss of altitude awareness
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper delayed pilot chute extraction while attempting to clear powerlines, ultimately deploying at approximately 120-150 feet AGL — far below survivable deployment altitude. The witness account and ground observers noted his flight became 'very flat compared to usual body position,' indicating he was flying at high AoA near stall trying to stretch his glide over the powerlines, which would have bled speed and available lift. Rather than deploying earlier with adequate altitude (accepting a landing short of or into the powerlines), he continued flying until he barely cleared them, at which point insufficient altitude remained for canopy inflation. The execution error was in failing to deploy at a survivable altitude — the PC was extracted too late for the canopy to fully inflate before ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity flight path selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper made a deliberate turn during wingsuit flight for the purpose of descending and passing close to a major ledge. This intentional flight path decision to fly in close proximity to terrain was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact. The decision to route the flight path close to the ledge was clearly and specifically inappropriate, as it eliminated the margin needed to survive any variation in performance, conditions, or execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / missed pull
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed too late, impacting trees at line stretch with no real canopy inflation. Video evidence and witness speculation indicate he lost altitude awareness while filming other jumpers, possibly missed the BOC handle on initial reach, and ultimately extracted the pilot chute too late to achieve a survivable opening altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited in a head-down position with one shoulder down and opposite leg kicking, indicating a failed exit execution from a technical exit point. The unstable exit in a wingsuit prevented recovery — the wingsuit trapped his arms, limiting his ability to regain stability. He tumbled from exit to impact without deploying his parachute (pilot chute reportedly still in pouch), resulting in fatal freefall impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states Anis 'left unstable and ended up impacting the cliff.' The unstable exit resulted in failure to achieve adequate flight and separation from the cliff, leading to cliff impact. The execution of the exit was the producing mechanism for the fatality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited an antenna in gusting winds of 18-22 mph (8-10 m/s) that were changing from crosswind to tailwind. The domain reference states few objects are good in winds >10 mph and no objects are good >20 mph. Antennas require careful wind evaluation, and these conditions were clearly excessive for a slider-down antenna jump. The strong, gusting crosswinds caused the hand-held PC to inflate prematurely and be blown behind the jumper, directly producing the PC-in-tow/entanglement that resulted in a closed-container impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature PC inflation on exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper held the PC by the mesh, allowing it to inflate prior to exit in the strong winds. An inflated PC in hand during exit in gusty crosswinds meant the jumper could not execute a proper throw - the wind blew the inflated PC behind the jumper into the burble area. This execution failure in managing the hand-held deployment directly contributed to the PC failing to generate extraction force in time, resulting in impact before line stretch was complete.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment while clearing ski
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper successfully released the stuck left ski but consumed critical altitude doing so. After clearing the malfunction and achieving a flying position, he was too low to deploy or arrest his descent. The execution of the ski release—while deliberate and ultimately successful—took too long relative to available altitude, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. The fatal mechanism was the failure to deploy the parachute at a survivable altitude, driven by the time consumed managing the equipment malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was observed to be unstable in freefall after exiting from the Nose at Murrenfluh. The unstable exit resulted in insufficient forward separation from the cliff, leading to impact with a ledge or the cliff face near the ground. The intended action was a stable slider-up cliff jump, but the physical execution of the exit failed to produce the stability and separation needed to clear the terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew proximity line beyond skill level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Mike had approximately 8 wingsuit BASE jumps and was flying Bispen, described as not a beginner or intermediate jump suitable only for very experienced wingsuit pilots. With only ~8 WS BASE jumps he was far below the experience level required for this object. The accepted progression norm requires at least 100 WS BASE jumps before proximity flying, and the domain reference explicitly warns that jumpers routinely overestimate their ability. His decision to fly this line with his experience level was the producing mechanism: he was observed flying slow and close to terrain, consistent with the slow-flight failure mode described in the domain reference where insufficient speed bleeds available lift and glide, leaving no margin to clear terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed and glide performance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: A witness observed Mike flying far below the other jumper and appearing to fly slowly near the rock feature. This is consistent with the recognized wingsuit failure pattern of flying at too high an angle of attack near stall, resulting in degraded glide performance and increasing sink rate. His inability to maintain adequate speed and separation from terrain led to an emergency pull at insufficient altitude, resulting in impact at line stretch. The execution failure in airspeed and flight path management was the direct mechanism of the fatal terrain impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's track did not separate him sufficiently from the wall despite a perfect exit. The High Nose is known to require BASE-specific tracking skill for adequate separation. The insufficient forward separation meant that when he experienced a 180° off-heading opening, he lacked the distance from the object needed to complete the heading correction before impacting the talus.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped unsuitable exit for first WS BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Leroy chose to jump the West wall, which had only 600ft to the talus and is described as a technical wingsuit jump, for his first wingsuit BASE jump. This exit point was wholly unsuitable for a first wingsuit BASE jump, requiring proximity flying skills and providing minimal margin. The decision to jump this specific exit directly produced the fatal outcome, as the inadequate altitude and positive/technical terrain left no room for the expected poor start arc performance of a first-time wingsuit BASE jumper.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of airspeed leading to late low deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Witnesses observed that Jimmy did not appear to have much forward speed for his wingsuit and was flying close to the stall point toward the end of his flight. This slow/near-stall flight is a recognized performance error in wingsuit BASE: it degrades glide performance, increases sink rate, enlarges the burble, and depletes available lift. The consequence was that he arrived at deployment altitude approximately 100m short of his planned opening area with insufficient speed and altitude, resulting in a deployment too low for the canopy to fully inflate before tree/terrain impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity flight path too close to building
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a flight path from an L-shaped building that put him on a glidepath very close to the building edge. This decision to fly a proximity line past the building structure was the producing mechanism for the fatal object strike, as the chosen trajectory did not provide adequate clearance from the building's L-shaped extension. The jumper's attempt to tuck his legs confirms the clearance was marginal by design rather than the result of an execution error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to correct off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The canopy opened approximately 120° off-heading with a half line twist. The narrative explicitly states there was no attempt to correct the heading, and Scott impacted the wall after 3-4 seconds of canopy flight. The failure to execute any heading correction — via rear risers, brakes, or any input — was the direct mechanism that produced the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Weak exit / poor separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Scott had a weak launch that produced very little separation from the wall, reducing the available time and distance to survive an off-heading opening. Poor exit technique directly contributed to the insufficient object clearance that made the uncorrected off-heading fatal.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted wingsuit-only jump with tracking suit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a cliff exit (Mighty Penis / Via Ferrata) that is described as a 'Wingsuit only jump' for 'experienced Wingsuiter only,' using only a tracking suit instead of a wingsuit. This decision to attempt a jump that required wingsuit-level glide performance with equipment incapable of providing it was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome: the jumper was unable to outtrack the last ledge and impacted terrain at terminal speed with nothing deployed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inability to avoid powerlines post-opening
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper opened with a 90-degree left off-heading facing the powerlines and impacted them approximately 4 seconds later. The narrative states it was impossible to know from the observers' viewpoint whether he had line twists preventing control, whether he misjudged his ability to fly over the powerlines, or whether some other factor prevented avoidance. There is insufficient information to determine whether this was a decision error (attempting to fly over rather than avoid) or a performance/skill-based error (inability to execute a correction due to line twists or other execution failure).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Fell before completing setup
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper fell from the object before completing their jump setup. The possible factors state 'Fell before setting up,' indicating the jumper failed to maintain safe positioning on the object during preparation, resulting in an unintended fall with equipment not properly configured for deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted line beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Geo chose to fly a proximity line over a saddle that required clearing terrain by a narrow margin (50-100m planned clearance). His close friend described him as a beginner pilot with only 1-2 years in the entire sport from first BASE jump to flying big wingsuits, with poor exits (classic head-high student exits), representing 'too much too soon.' The decision to attempt a terrain-clearance proximity line with his actual skill level was clearly and specifically inappropriate — the line demanded performance he could not reliably produce, and impact occurred at the saddle he intended to clear.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Overly steep exit leading to unrecoverable flight path
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's exit was too steep and went past vertical, as indicated by photos taken from the edge. This excessive pitch resulted in a flight path that brought the jumper into contact with the wall approximately 4 seconds after exit. The narrator, an experienced wingsuit jumper, describes having experienced a similar overly steep exit caused by expedition boots tipping the body slightly past vertical, and recovered through a hard arch — indicating this was an execution/technique error in exit body position rather than a deliberate decision to exit steeply.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to extract pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper made five attempts to locate and grasp the BOC-mounted pilot chute but was unable to extract it, going unstable with each attempt. The narrative indicates the rig was not custom-fitted to his body dimensions, causing the BOC to shift out of reach in freefall position. This execution failure—inability to physically reach and deploy the pilot chute—directly produced the total malfunction and fatal ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a bad exit described as sideways, which prevented him from establishing effective wingsuit flight. This unstable exit was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome, as he was unable to fly out of the narrowing gully and impacted terrain near the exit point at subterminal speed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate object selection for experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: An inexperienced wingsuit pilot chose (or accepted the group's choice of) a technical wingsuit jump requiring quick, effective flight to clear a narrowing gully. The domain reference establishes that wingsuit BASE requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 BASE jumps, with exits blending seamlessly into tracks, and that objects with mandatory performance requirements (flying out of confining terrain) demand commensurate skill. Selecting this technical object as an inexperienced wingsuit pilot was a clearly inappropriate decision independent of execution quality, as even a moderately imperfect exit at this site was non-survivable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle entanglement with body during rigging
L1_unsafe_acts.description: While rigging his bridle and static line apparatus, Stimson had the bridle wrapped around one of his lower extremities. Upon exit, the bridle pulled tight, extracted the pins, and caused the canopy to wrap around his body, resulting in a total malfunction and fatal impact. The jumper intended to perform a routine static line jump but failed in the physical execution of properly clearing the bridle from his body before exiting.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a tracking jump from a cliff with a 10-second delay but did not achieve sufficient forward separation from the wall. Upon opening with a 180-degree off-heading, she struck the wall, indicating that her tracking performance and/or body position at deployment failed to create adequate distance to survive an off-heading opening. The possible factors note insufficient forward separation on exit and late pilot chute extraction, both execution failures during an otherwise routine slider-up cliff jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment from aerials
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Anton performed a triple pike into front flips, remaining in rotation until 9-10 seconds on a 12-second rock drop. He came out of rotation still facing the wall and did not extract the pilot chute until approximately the 12-second mark, leaving zero time or separation to survive the resulting 180-degree off-heading opening. The aerials were intentional but the execution failed: he remained in rotation too long, lost altitude awareness, and deployed too late with insufficient object separation to survive any off-heading.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of object strike
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper died from object strike under canopy, with possible factors being a 180° off-heading with slow reaction time and/or insufficient forward separation on exit. However, there were no witnesses to the exit or deployment, and the narrative explicitly states uncertainty about what occurred — whether it was a 180° off-heading with slow reaction, a line-over, or insufficient separation. There is insufficient information to distinguish between a decision error and a performance error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient exit push / unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to clear a steep ledge 60-80m below the exit that required a serious launch to clear. Whether due to slipping on unstable terrain or an insufficiently forceful exit, the jumper struck the ledge, losing his helmet and camera on impact. The severe head/neck injuries from the ledge strike, untouched toggles, and subsequent uncontrolled flight into a steep gorge indicate the ledge impact was the producing mechanism of the fatality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and inadequate tracking
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Herbert exited head-down and, after briefly stabilizing, made an unintentional barrel-roll movement to the right. This unstable exit and subsequent loss of control resulted in insufficient forward separation from the cliff. He opened very low and close to the wall, leading to immediate object strike under canopy. The execution failures in exit stability and separation directly produced the fatal proximity to the cliff at opening.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed PC location - muscle memory error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reached for a leg pouch (his habitual pull location for the Vampire 3) instead of the BOC handle on the Phantom Edge. After 2-3 unsuccessful attempts at the wrong location, he remembered the correct BOC position and deployed, but the delay consumed the remaining altitude, resulting in ground impact during line extension. This was a memory/habit interference error in execution rather than a decision error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Weak/improper PC deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper pitched the pilot chute forward and close to her head rather than out to the side at approximately 45 degrees. The exit point coordinator observed the pitch looked different than normal, appearing closer to her head and forward. This weak, improperly directed PC toss, combined with the crosswind, allowed the bridle to wrap around the helmet-mounted GoPro camera, resulting in a PC-in-tow/entanglement that consumed the available altitude on a 300ft building.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper became unstable immediately after exit and entered a prolonged spin and roll from which he could not recover. This execution failure — an unstable wingsuit exit that he was unable to correct — consumed the altitude needed for safe deployment. He eventually deployed but too late, striking a ledge before canopy inflation, with the cause of death being low pull resulting from the sustained instability.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit causing ledge strike
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Gary pushed off hard from the exit platform but failed to maintain proper head-high body attitude, resulting in an uncontrolled head-down rotation with excessive speed. This unstable exit caused him to strike a large outcrop/ledge shortly after exit, resulting in severe trauma and subterminal impact. The witness account describes him rotating head down faster than usual, consistent with a technique/execution error on exit rather than a flawed decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Held PC after extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted the PC from the BOC during a gainer but held onto it rather than immediately releasing it into clean air. This brief hold allowed the bridle to extend to full stretch behind him while still rotating, enabling his foot to pass through the bridle loop. The narrative states he had never been observed doing this before, indicating it was an unintended execution error rather than a deliberate technique choice.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to extract pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was observed falling fast with arms on his back, apparently trying to reach his pilot chute in his leg pouch but unable to extract it before impact. He impacted at full speed with the PC still in the container. This is a execution failure in the physical act of deploying the parachute — the jumper intended to pull but could not successfully locate or extract the PC from the leg pouch.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking and late deployment
