L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was not stable enough after exit, did not track well, and extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in insufficient separation from the cliff. The canopy opened only upon or after wall impact. The execution failures in tracking and deployment timing directly produced the fatal object strike, despite the jumper having selected an appropriate course of action (slider-up cliff jump with BOC deployment).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed-glide near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Mirko's start was described as 'a bit slow' and the narrative indicates he likely found himself lower than expected when approaching the black ridge. His loss of speed and glide during wingsuit flight near terrain left him without sufficient altitude or energy to clear the ridge or execute the alternate right-turn escape route, resulting in impact with the wall during an emergency deployment attempt.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed terrain avoidance decision
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states that the left line required a quick decision after turning at the pillar, as the black ridge stood 'like a fence.' Mirko likely took too long to decide whether he could clear the ridge or needed to turn right. By the time he committed to a course of action, his ability to fly the alternate escape line had been lost, placing him in an unrecoverable position near terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor tracking direction and pull location
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper tracked toward the right side of the cliff where the wall height is lower and pillars are present, reducing available clearance. He then deployed his parachute close to the wall in this area of reduced separation, leaving insufficient margin to survive the line twists and off-heading opening that followed. The tracking direction and pull location constituted execution errors - a correctly executed track away from the cliff with deployment at adequate separation would have provided survivable distance even with the line twists and off-heading.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of incapacitation or error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was observed under canopy without applying any inputs, with brakes still stowed, spiraling into terrain. The narrative does not explain why the jumper failed to fly the canopy after deployment. The possible factors list references a 180 off-heading, clipping a ledge after exiting, and an emergency low pull, suggesting a sequence involving object strike during freefall that may have incapacitated the jumper. However, the narrative provides insufficient detail to determine whether the root active failure was a decision error (e.g., jumping in low-level clouds/fog, proximity flying in inadequate conditions) or a performance error (e.g., poor exit leading to ledge strike, failure to track adequately). The cause of death is listed as impact in terminal freefall, yet the jumper was found under canopy, creating ambiguity about the fatal mechanism.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate equipment for application
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used lightweight 400lb Dacron lines on a slider-up terminal cliff jump with a tracking suit at full-speed deployment, while being a heavy jumper. The narrative explicitly identifies multiple line failure on opening as the cause of death, attributing it to the combination of lightweight lines insufficient for the structural demands of the application (heavy jumper, high-airspeed slider-up opening). The decision to use equipment with inadequate structural margins for this specific loading scenario was the producing mechanism for the fatal malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late/failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute in time, resulting in deployment at fatally low altitude. The narrative states he had difficulty finding the pilot chute and was also too low when he began thinking about pulling. Impact occurred just after line stretch, indicating the deployment sequence was initiated far too late for the canopy to inflate and decelerate.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late PC deployment from gainer
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a gainer (aerial) from a span and extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in a PC/bridle entanglement (PC in tow) with insufficient altitude remaining to achieve canopy deployment. The cause of death was low pull, indicating the deployment sequence was initiated too late and/or the execution of the PC toss from the aerobatic maneuver was inadequate, resulting in impact with only the pilot chute out.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Selected unsuitable exit point
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to exit from a pillar he had just scouted for the first time, rather than the established ('normal') exit point. The update describes the area as 'severe technical, non adapt and dangerous for beginners' with only a 200m rock drop from that location. With only 50 wingsuit BASE jumps and 2 BASE seasons, selecting this unfamiliar, highly technical exit point for a wingsuit flight was a clearly inappropriate decision that produced the fatal outcome — he clipped a ledge shortly after exit and impacted in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Witnesses reported the jumper had a stable exit but 'just did not get her track on,' resulting in insufficient forward separation from the cliff. This failure to establish an effective track in her tracking suit was a performance/execution error — the intended action (track away from the cliff) was not achieved, leading to proximity to the ledge at deployment altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Terrain flight execution error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Rob was proximity flying along sloppy terrain and 'caught himself without an out,' indicating he failed to maintain adequate separation or energy margin during terrain flight. The narrative states 'he made a mistake,' pointing to an execution failure rather than a decision error. He impacted terrain in terminal freefall with no deployment, consistent with entering a no-pull zone from which recovery was impossible.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew technical line beyond WS BASE experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Antoine attempted his 3rd wingsuit BASE jump on a very technical line involving a long turn and passing under power lines. The domain reference establishes that wingsuit BASE requires at least 100 wingsuit skydives and 100 regular BASE jumps before a first wingsuit BASE jump, with extensive progression thereafter. Choosing a line requiring precise turning and terrain navigation with power lines on only his 3rd WS BASE jump was a decision that was clearly and specifically inadequate regardless of execution quality — the technical demands of this line far exceeded what could be safely managed at this experience level.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted the pilot chute too late during a slider-down cliff jump, resulting in the canopy opening too low to arrest descent adequately. The witness observed the parachute appeared to open fully but only when the jumper was approximately 3/4 of the way down the cliff. The deployment timing—a physical execution task—was insufficient for the available altitude, constituting a performance error rather than a decision error, as the jump could have been survived with a timely pull.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and extract pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper made four attempts to locate and extract the BOC-stowed pilot chute but failed on each attempt, resulting in ground impact in a stable box position with no canopy deployment. The execution of the pull sequence failed despite the jumper's clear intent to deploy, constituting a performance/skill-based error in extracting the PC from the BOC pocket.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with delayed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Sergio had a slightly unstable exit that turned his body to the right. He remained in this off-axis position for several seconds before slowly recovering and initiating his track, consuming critical freefall time needed for object separation. This delayed recovery and late initiation of tracking directly resulted in insufficient separation from the cliff when he deployed at approximately 7 seconds, leading to impact with the ledge during canopy opening.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped object beyond experience level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had only 20-50 BASE jumps and attempted a slider-up cliff exit (High Nose) that is classified as requiring wingsuit or experienced tracker skills. The narrative explicitly states this exit requires experienced tracking ability, and the jumper's low jump count indicates insufficient experience for this object. The decision to jump this specific object was clearly inappropriate for the jumper's experience level, and this decision produced the fatal outcome through insufficient object separation leading to object strike after a 180° off-heading.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate tracking and separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve sufficient forward separation from the cliff through tracking, and experienced a 180° off-heading opening, resulting in object strike under canopy. The insufficient separation meant that even a moderate off-heading would have been life-threatening, and the 180° off-heading was fatal.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve sufficient forward separation on exit from the cliff, as noted in the possible factors. This insufficient push/separation, combined with a 180-degree off-heading opening, resulted in object strike with the wall. The jumper survived the initial impact but died when the canopy dislodged after 30 minutes, causing a fall of up to 60 metres.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of no deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted terrain in terminal freefall while flying a wingsuit. His canopy was found with slider attached and brakes stowed, and witnesses could not confirm whether the impact occurred before any pull attempt, during a late pull, a failed pull attempt, or due to another malfunction. The narrative explicitly states 'we don't know what exactly happened: no pull / late pull / failed attempt to pull / other malfunction,' making it impossible to distinguish between a decision error (e.g., delayed pull decision due to loss of altitude awareness) and a performance error (e.g., failed PC extraction or late execution).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Misjudged terrain clearance during turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a tight proximity line and attempted to clear a large, wide ledge. Witnesses believe he realized at the last second he would not clear it and initiated a sharp turn. The turn was insufficient to avoid the ledge, resulting in terrain impact. The jumper had only ~12 jumps on the Vampire 4 wingsuit and had not practiced sharp turning maneuvers in it, despite being highly skilled in his previous S-Fly suit. The execution failure was in misjudging the clearance margin and being unable to execute the emergency turn with adequate precision in the relatively unfamiliar suit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to cliff strike
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and turned left into the cliff wall, impacting after approximately 4 seconds of freefall. The narrative and possible factors indicate an unstable exit that resulted in the jumper failing to establish controlled flight away from the wall. The canopy opened only after impact, confirming the jumper never recovered from the failed exit to achieve adequate separation from the terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Terrain strike during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying aggressively in 'attack mode' during a proximity wingsuit flight along a technical left-hand line near terrain. While executing a left-hand turn around a terrain feature, he impacted a grass field near a chalet, dying from terminal freefall impact. The cause of death (impact in terminal freefall with no parachute deployment) indicates a failure in execution during the proximity flight — either misjudging clearance from terrain, losing adequate speed/lift margin during the turn, or encountering conditions that degraded flight performance during the maneuver. The jumper chose a line he was experienced with and conditions were good; the fatal outcome resulted from how the flight was executed rather than from the decision to fly the line.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Off-heading opening with line twists
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed his parachute and experienced a 160° off-heading opening with multiple line twists. While under canopy with line twists preventing effective heading correction, the canopy flew toward the wall resulting in multiple object strikes and fatal impact. The off-heading with line twists was the direct mechanism producing the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature deployment near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed his parachute at a point approximately 30m after and 30m below a ledge while flying proximity to terrain on his first jump at this cliff. Witnesses assessed that the deployment location was driven by panic or stress, and that deploying at this point left insufficient clearance from the wall to survive an off-heading. The decision to deploy at this specific point near the wall, rather than continuing flight to gain more separation, was the producing mechanism that placed him in a position where the off-heading was fatal rather than recoverable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown deployment failure cause
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a fatal deployment malfunction where the pilot chute extracted only approximately 10 feet of canopy that remained long and thin without inflating, resulting in full-speed impact. The updated report identifies the pilot chute was entangled with a stash bag compression cord. While this points to either a packing/rigging error or a gear check omission, the narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the jumper made a specific decision error (e.g., deliberately rigging the stash bag cord in a hazardous configuration) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., failing to properly secure or remove the compression cord during packing). The jumpers did not perform mutual gear checks, but a gear check omission is a failure to detect an existing problem rather than the producing mechanism itself.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and deploy PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate the pilot chute from the BOC pocket in time and impacted the ground with nothing deployed. The narrative states she was unable to pull the PC at sufficient altitude, indicating an execution failure during the deployment sequence rather than a decision not to deploy. The ill-fitting Vampire 4 wingsuit likely contributed to difficulty reaching the BOC, but the fatal mechanism was the failed execution of the pull.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient speed/glide near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit in proximity to terrain and was 'slowly closing his window of safety fast,' indicating a progressive loss of speed and glide performance during the flight. He impacted the talus he was attempting to fly over, demonstrating that his execution of the proximity line failed to maintain adequate speed and separation from terrain. The narrative describes a gradual degradation in flight performance rather than a flawed plan — he was attempting a line that was within his intended progression but failed to maintain the energy management required to clear the terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide in wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper lost speed and began sinking out during the second half of his wingsuit flight, indicating he was not maintaining adequate airspeed and flew into or near a stall condition. Fellow jumpers had noted in weeks prior that he was not maximizing the performance of his recently changed wingsuit. This loss of speed/glide resulted in insufficient altitude for canopy deployment and flight, leading to what was effectively a terminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After losing speed and sinking out, the jumper deployed his pilot chute too late. While the canopy did inflate, it opened with a 140+ degree off-heading at an altitude insufficient to correct or land safely, resulting in impact into a gully behind a cliff band.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper pushed off harder than normal from the ramp, resulting in a head-down position with feet exceeding vertical with great amplitude. He was unable to recover stable flight from this unstable exit, entered a spin, and impacted a ledge. The exit technique execution was the direct producing mechanism — the intended action was a controlled wingsuit exit, but the physical execution (excessive push force, resulting head-down attitude) was inadequate.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to maintain adequate speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was proximity flying along a wall in a Vampire 4 wingsuit and impacted a ledge after 10 seconds of flight. The possible factors list 'Loss of speed-glide,' indicating the jumper failed to maintain adequate airspeed and glide performance during execution. The jumper's flight path intersected terrain due to insufficient speed management, resulting in terminal freefall impact with a ledge.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment after performance degradation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Carl's wingsuit performance rapidly degraded with very little forward speed and high fall rate, yet he had plenty of altitude to deploy at that point and did not. He continued flying and eventually extracted the pilot chute very low over trees at approximately 100m above ground, resulting in insufficient altitude for canopy inflation and terminal impact. The failure was in execution: he failed to deploy the parachute when altitude was available despite the clearly degraded flight conditions, indicating an attention/monitoring failure regarding altitude and the need to abandon the flight plan.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was found at the base of the antenna with the canopy extracted but insufficient altitude remaining for inflation. The possible factors indicate the pilot chute was extracted too late, constituting a performance/execution error in deployment timing from a slider-down antenna jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable/poor wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted the wall near the exit point at subterminal speed, indicating a failed wingsuit start. On his first jump he had been noted for 'slightly bent legs' which was discussed as a concern, and on the fatal second jump witnesses saw him hit the cliff shortly after exit. This indicates a performance failure during the exit — likely an insufficient or unstable launch that prevented the wingsuit from generating adequate lift and separation from the cliff face.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with insufficient push
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited unstably and failed to generate sufficient push to clear a known small outcropping approximately 1 second after exit. She clipped a bush/ledge, tumbled into the wall, and impacted the cliff face at subterminal speed. The exit required a decent push to clear the outcropping, and the execution was insufficient to achieve the necessary separation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flying proximity line in excessive wind
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper committed to a proximity flying line along a ridge in strong headwind conditions that reduced airspeed and available lift, making terrain clearance physically impossible. The narrative explicitly states the wind's importance was 'trivialized by our confidence in our big suits,' the line had never been flown before, and the co-jumper directly experienced the 'strong head wind and subsequent lack of speed' that forced them to bail into the canyon. Flying a never-flown proximity line in strong winds that degraded wingsuit performance below what was needed to clear terrain was a decision that was clearly and specifically inadequate for the conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper did not appear to have attempted to pull his pilot chute despite approximately 30 seconds of flight time before impacting the ridge. Whatever the reason for the non-deployment — loss of altitude awareness, task saturation, or inability to recognize the need to abort — the failure to deploy the parachute was the direct mechanism of the fatal impact in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited from a technical under-hung exit point using a wingsuit and went head-down immediately after leaving the ramp, failing to achieve a stable flying position. This unstable exit resulted in insufficient forward separation and direct impact with a ledge below the exit point. The narrative indicates the exit was a push-off rather than the preferred running/tracking exit for this technical site, and the jumper failed to maintain the body position necessary to initiate wingsuit flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient speed/glide over ridge
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper selected a line over a nearby ridge but was visibly considerably lower than a second jumper flying the same line, indicating he failed to maintain adequate speed and glide performance during the flight. The possible factors list 'Loss of speed-glide' and the cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, consistent with the wingsuit losing performance and the jumper being unable to clear or separate from terrain. The decision to fly the ridge line was shared by at least one other jumper who completed it successfully, indicating the line itself was flyable and the failure was in execution rather than plan selection.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Under-rotated aerial causing unstable deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Luke attempted a double gainer but under-rotated, completing only approximately 1.5 rotations. This left him in an unstable, back-to-earth position from which he pitched the pilot chute while twisting his torso to face the ground. The resulting body orientation and deployment from his back led to the canopy snagging on his belly camera, causing a total malfunction (failure to inflate) and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Incorrect bridle routing
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper routed his bridle through the lateral flap of his harness (the strap connecting the leg harness to the back of the container), which prevented the canopy from deploying when the PCA was executed. This rigging error during setup produced a pilot chute in tow / total malfunction that was unrecoverable at the jump altitude, directly causing impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's exit was unstable and deviated to the left, putting him too close to the wall. This execution failure directly resulted in subterminal impact with terrain near the exit point a few seconds later.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity flying beyond experience level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit proximity line too close to terrain, resulting in terrain impact in terminal freefall with no deployment attempt. The narrative explicitly states the jumper had no experience in proximity flight and was fairly new to wingsuiting, making the decision to fly a close proximity line a clear and specific departure from accepted progression norms. The decision to fly that line at that distance from terrain was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying very low to make the first turn and after the turn had put himself too low to fly out safely, clipping an outcrop that flipped him upside down. This indicates a failure in execution of the proximity line — specifically, inadequate speed/glide management through the turn resulted in insufficient altitude and separation to clear terrain. The jumper's flight technique failed to maintain the energy and trajectory needed to safely navigate the chosen line.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew proximity line beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flight line that required crossing over a ridge ('the First house of card'). He impacted the terrain in terminal freefall with nothing deployed, indicating he committed to a line that required more glide performance than was available. The possible factors of 'loss of speed-glide' and 'proximity flying' combined with the narrative's emphasis on knowing limits indicate the chosen flight path was clearly inappropriate given the performance margin available.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Altitude/terrain misjudgment during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew a known wingsuit proximity line but had a 'wrong appreciation of the height' over a grassy dome feature along the flight path, resulting in insufficient clearance. This was an execution-level error in terrain awareness and altitude management during proximity flying, not a flawed decision about which line to fly. The misjudgment led to terrain impact at terminal speed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed terrain avoidance maneuver
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying near the wall and encountered a more prominent section of terrain. He initiated a turn to avoid it but executed the turn too sharply, causing the wingsuit to roll and pitch head-down. He was unable to recover from this attitude and impacted the slope in terminal freefall. The intended action (turning to avoid terrain) was correct, but the execution was flawed — the turn was too aggressive, inducing loss of control.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew proximity line beyond skill level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Dan was described as 'only a novice wingsuit Proxy flyer' who attempted a proximity line he failed to clear. The decision to fly a proximity line near trees as a novice proxy flyer was clearly and specifically inappropriate given his acknowledged lack of proximity flying experience, and this decision directly produced the fatal terrain impact. He was found in flight mode with no pull attempt, indicating the terrain strike resulted from the chosen flight path rather than an execution error during deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew proximity line into unknown terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The deceased chose to fly a proximity line into a crack/gully without adequate knowledge of where it led. He turned right into a dead-end crack that was approximately 20 meters from the correct line. Flying proximity to terrain features that the jumper cannot see around ('turning around blind features') without knowing exactly what is around the corner is a clearly inappropriate decision, as the jumper committed to a line with no escape route into terrain he had not verified, directly producing the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of altitude awareness during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was proxy flying and lost height awareness, resulting in impact with talus in terminal freefall without deploying the parachute. The narrative explicitly states he 'lost height awareness,' indicating an attention/monitoring failure during execution of the proximity flight rather than a flawed decision to attempt the flight itself.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose exit beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump an exit described as one of the top 3 most advanced wingsuit jumps in the world, which has killed even highly experienced wingsuit pilots with hundreds of WS BASE jumps. The narrative explicitly states the exit is 'not forgiving' and requires flawless exit and flying skills. The decision to attempt this specific exit was clearly and specifically inappropriate given the jumper's apparent skill level, as evidenced by the 'too steep (unskilled)' exit characterization. The decision itself — selecting this particular exit — was the producing mechanism, as even correct execution of a too-steep exit from this unforgiving launch would leave insufficient margin for recovery.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unskilled steep exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly states the exit was 'too steep (unskilled)' and the jumper was unable to get the wingsuit flying in time. The jumper failed to execute a proper wingsuit exit — exiting at too steep an angle prevented building sufficient airspeed for flight before terrain impact, resulting in subterminal impact with nothing deployed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature wingsuit BASE attempt
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted wingsuit BASE jumping with only 22 prior BASE jumps (10 slider-down before the trip, plus 12 in the valley), far below the accepted minimum of 100 regular BASE jumps before first wingsuit BASE. With only 10 slider-down jumps and no reported slider-up non-wingsuit cliff experience, the jumper lacked the foundational skills for wingsuit BASE. This decision to attempt wingsuit BASE at this experience level was a clear and specific departure from accepted progression norms.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unsuitable object selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper selected Yellow Ocean (350m rock drop, 425m to landing area) for a wingsuit flight. This exit lacks sufficient altitude for wingsuit flight, particularly for a novice wingsuit BASE jumper who would have a large start arc and poor speed management. The jumper assumed it was suitable for beginners without obtaining local site information or registering with the SBA, ignoring advice from experienced jumpers in the valley.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Head-high slow flight to stall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew the wingsuit in a very head-high, slow configuration likely at or near stall, as observed by witnesses. This is a recognized fatal error pattern in wingsuit BASE: flying too slowly bleeds energy, increases sink rate, enlarges the burble, and prevents effective deployment. The resulting loss of speed and glide led to insufficient altitude for canopy deployment, with the canopy reaching line stretch simultaneously with ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of control during turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During a competitive wingsuit proximity flight, the jumper lost control after the first gate while setting up for the second gate. He slipped sideways, went up on his wingtip turning right, and lost speed and altitude, impacting trees in terminal freefall. The execution of the turn was the direct mechanism — the jumper failed to maintain stable flight and adequate airspeed through the maneuver.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate site for new wingsuit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a new, larger wingsuit (R-Bird) for the first time from Via Ferrata, which is explicitly described as 'Not a Beginners Wing suit Exit.' This was a clear departure from his own stated plan to test the R-Bird at Brento (a recognized beginner wingsuit exit). The decision to fly an unfamiliar, larger suit from a non-beginner exit was the producing mechanism: the site's demands exceeded what could be managed on a first flight in the new suit, and the resulting uncontrolled flight led to subterminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to uncontrolled flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited unstable and never recovered controlled flight before impacting talus. Other jumpers had previously observed that his exits in his smaller suit (Prodigy) were consistently too head-high and that he was not achieving adequate airspeed on exit. This execution deficiency — an exit technique that failed to establish controlled wingsuit flight — was the direct mechanism producing the fatal terrain impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Missed pilot chute grab
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to deploy his pilot chute but missed on his first attempt to locate and extract it from the BOC. Although he found it on a subsequent attempt, the delay meant the canopy reached line stretch at or after ground impact. This was an execution error in a time-critical deployment sequence from a slider-down cliff jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper tracked for too long and deployed his pilot chute right before impact, resulting in lines and canopy out but no inflation. The witness account and video review indicate he likely lost altitude awareness or became fixated on an object (a tree), failing to monitor altitude and initiate deployment at a survivable height. The jump plan itself (a 2-way with a roll maneuver from an antenna) was not inherently the producing mechanism; rather, the failure was in execution—specifically, the failure to deploy at the correct altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to deploy her pilot chute at a survivable altitude. The narrative states she reached back to pull around 15 seconds into the flight but was 'much too low and impacted before she threw her pilot chute.' This indicates a failure to monitor altitude during wingsuit flight, resulting in initiating the deployment sequence too late to complete it before ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor wingsuit flight performance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper 'did not build up much forward speed and was not out flying the talus,' indicating inadequate wingsuit flight technique. The poor flight performance — likely flying too slowly at too high an angle of attack — reduced her effective glide and contributed to her being too close to terrain when she finally attempted deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment after failed aerial
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a double gainer but stalled the second rotation. By the time he recovered to belly-to-earth orientation, he had consumed too much altitude and deployed too late. The canopy was extracted but lines were still stowed at impact, indicating insufficient altitude remained for the deployment sequence to complete. The fatal mechanism was the failure to maintain altitude awareness and deploy in time during the failed aerial maneuver.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to initiate canopy turn
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a straight, on-heading opening facing Lauterbrunnen (parallel to the cliff), the jumper failed to turn the canopy away from the cliff face. The narrative explicitly states 'she didn't turn the canopy' despite having a functioning, on-heading parachute. This failure to execute a necessary heading correction or object-avoidance maneuver resulted in continued flight parallel to the cliff and fatal impact with the talus.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued toward ridge after altitude loss
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the initial over-rotation (~100° instead of 70°) left the jumper already low, he made a conscious decision to turn sharp right and attempt to fly over the ridge rather than deploying or flying to open airspace. This second decision committed him to a flight path with insufficient altitude to clear the terrain, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. The companion's account explicitly frames this as a sequential decision: he had margin to survive the first error but the decision to still attempt the ridge crossing was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to achieve adequate separation and correct off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper opened with a 180-degree off-heading and line twists, with insufficient separation from the cliff. The possible factors indicate the jumper failed to track adequately for object separation and/or failed to correct the off-heading in time, resulting in fatal object strike under canopy. With 30 BASE jumps and 1 season of experience at a slider-up cliff, the execution of tracking and deployment produced inadequate separation, and the off-heading with line twists prevented effective correction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late or failed deployment during aerial
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a gainer from an awkward exit position that likely caused slow over-rotation or stall. The pilot chute was extracted but the pins were not pulled, indicating either a late pull (insufficient altitude remaining) or bridle entanglement with the stash bag's elastic strap. The execution of the aerial and subsequent deployment attempt failed to produce a functioning parachute before impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate riser release technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After correcting a 180° off-heading with rear riser input, the jumper released the riser in a manner that caused the canopy to dive forward and surge him into an underhung portion of the cliff. The abrupt release of rear riser input after a full heading correction generated a forward surge that drove the jumper into the rock face. This is a technique/execution error in the transition from heading correction back to normal flight, rather than a decision error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited unstable from a cliff and failed to achieve adequate forward separation, impacting the wall only a few seconds after exit. The unstable exit was a performance/execution error that directly produced the fatal outcome by preventing the jumper from tracking away from the object.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment decision
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued flying his wingsuit despite progressively losing altitude and being clearly too low to clear the terrain ahead. Video footage confirmed he was already low after exit and was 'slowly and regularly losing altitude' throughout the flight. He had 'plenty of time to open safely' but chose to continue flying until approximately 20m from the ground, at which point deployment was too late. This was a decision error — the jumper recognized or should have recognized the deteriorating situation over an extended period but chose not to deploy, making the delayed deployment the producing mechanism of the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in excessive wind beyond site limits
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly states that winds were 'beyond the measures they have in place for this site' - meaning the jumper chose to jump when wind conditions exceeded the established site safety limits. This decision to jump in conditions that violated site rules was the producing mechanism: excessive wind at a cliff object degrades tracking performance, contributes to unstable body position at deployment, and causes off-heading openings with insufficient separation, directly producing the fatal object strike under canopy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable track and poor deployment body position
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper had a 'poor unstable track' followed by pulling 'having a bad body position,' resulting in line twists and a 180 off-heading. Even given the wind conditions, the execution failures in tracking and deployment body position were the direct mechanism producing insufficient object separation and the off-heading opening that led to cliff strike. The jumper was also 'slow to react' to the off-heading/line twists, failing to execute timely heading correction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Abraham was flying a technical wingsuit line near terrain and lost speed and glide performance, resulting in insufficient clearance to pass the Zebrata/climbing wall. The narrative indicates he experienced a loss of speed-glide during proximity flying, consistent with flying too slowly or at too high an angle of attack, which degraded his available lift and glide ratio to the point where terrain clearance was impossible.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late emergency deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Abraham attempted an emergency deployment just before reaching the terrain obstacle but extracted the pilot chute too late for the canopy to inflate before impact. The panic pull at the last moment indicates he recognized the situation but was unable to execute a timely deployment, resulting in impact in terminal freefall with no functioning canopy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient track/separation from cliff
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a running exit from a 450m cliff in slick configuration but failed to achieve adequate forward separation through tracking. The jumper impacted a ledge 250-300m below the exit point, described as hitting 'in the middle of the ledge,' indicating insufficient horizontal distance gained during freefall. Witnesses described the track/projection as 'too poor' and 'weak.'

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: While flying Line 2 at Brevent, Shaun exposed too much of the top surface of his wing to the relative wind during a transition from a hard dive, creating a downward force. His abrupt correction to angle of attack resulted in a high-speed stall and loss of stability, leading to an unrecoverable tumble to impact. The execution errors were in managing the dive-to-flight transition — specifically the over-exposure of the wing's top surface and the abrupt AoA correction — rather than in the decision to fly the line itself.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit / slip
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper slipped on the wet, muddy exit point during a wingsuit launch, immediately striking positive cliff terrain. The slip resulted in an unrecoverable tumble and subterminal impact near the exit. The execution failure (loss of footing) was the direct mechanism that produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit / failed start
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Despite a strong push, Alex's trajectory did not match his intended flight path and he was almost immediately in a 'head low, left bank' body position. This unstable exit failed to achieve the efficient start required to clear the ledge below the exit point. His attempted correction (dropping the leg wing aggressively) stalled the suit and induced a flat spin, resulting in ledge impact at subterminal altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide in flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit proximity line that 'demands good control and does not allow loss of flight.' He experienced a loss of speed-glide during the flight, resulting in insufficient performance to clear terrain (crête de la Chalavreu), leading to impact in terminal freefall. The chosen line required sustained adequate flight performance, and the jumper failed to maintain the necessary speed and glide to complete it.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wingsuit stall without recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Witnesses reported the jumper stalled his wingsuit approximately 600 meters before the entry to a crack feature and was unable to recover, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. This indicates a performance failure in airspeed/AoA management during proximity flight — the jumper lost speed and available lift, entered a stall condition, and failed to recover to controlled flight. The domain reference identifies flying too slowly and stalling as a known execution failure mode in wingsuit BASE, where speed loss is easy and recovery requires altitude that may not be available near terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to abort proximity line at decision point
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued flying a proximity line toward a ledge despite losing speed/glide, failing to abort at the point where safe clearance was still possible. The narrative explicitly states that the jumper could have aborted and flown away from the ledge at the right time to gain altitude, but he continued to fly and when he realized he would not make it over, it was too late. The lesson cited by the eyewitness is having a reference point where you abort your line or pull. This was a decision to continue a committed proximity line past the abort point, not an execution error — the jumper flew the suit as intended but chose to press on rather than abort when adequate margin still existed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed terrain flight execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to fly a left line too early, misjudging the start arc which was longer than anticipated. This execution error during proximity wingsuit flight resulted in insufficient altitude/separation from terrain, leading to high-speed impact. The jumper intended to fly the line but failed in the cognitive execution of timing the transition, misreading the start arc performance relative to the terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit push
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly states that the exit commanded a strong push, the first jumper pushed hard and cleared the terrain, but Donald did not push hard enough. At 90kg he did not generate sufficient speed to clear the first ledge at approximately 100 meters / 4.5 seconds from the exit. The intended action was to clear the terrain, but the physical execution of the exit was insufficient, resulting in impact with the ledge.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a known tracking suit line near terrain and failed to deploy the pilot chute with sufficient altitude remaining. The narrative states he realized he was low and attempted to pitch, but there was not sufficient altitude for deployment, indicating a failure in altitude awareness and deployment timing during execution of the flight rather than a flawed plan selection.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate static line rigging method
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Channing chose to rig his static line using improvised methods that departed from accepted practice in multiple ways: he used a spectra line and rock as an anchor, employed two pieces of break-cord tied together rather than a single proper length, attached the static line connections far toward the end of the bridle (increasing dynamic loading), used old/degraded break-cord that had been torn apart rather than cut, and elected not to use a pilot chute as backup. These were conscious rigging decisions that collectively created a system prone to failure. The primary knot slipped free and the secondary break-cord broke prematurely at the intermediary knot, resulting in the canopy not reaching full line stretch and a closed-container impact from 140 feet.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: No pilot chute backup
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Channing deliberately chose to static line without a pilot chute for backup. Accepted practice calls for the PC to never be stowed during a static line jump, as it serves as a last line of defense if the static line system fails. Had a pilot chute been available, it could have extracted the canopy after the static line rigging failed. This decision removed the only remaining means of canopy deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Rigging/packing execution error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Bryan constructed his own bridle with a floating bottom pin configuration and packed/rigged the system such that the bottom pin locked in the closing loop, resulting in a total malfunction (closed container impact). The intact closing loop was found large enough to admit the pin eye, and various malfunction modes were discovered on similarly configured containers. The pin lock prevented canopy extraction despite normal PC inflation and full bridle extension, directly producing the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in impact at line stretch. The possible factors explicitly cite 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late' and 'Altitude awareness,' indicating a failure in execution timing rather than a deliberate decision to pull at that altitude. The jumper had sufficient experience (250 BASE jumps, 700 skydives) and appropriate equipment for a slider-up tracking suit jump, suggesting the intended course of action was sound but execution of the deployment was fatally delayed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late separation and deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jim separated from the burning parachute too low, and although he deployed his normal BASE canopy normally, there was insufficient altitude for it to inflate before impact. The narrative states he 'extracted pilot chute too late' — the execution of the separation and deployment sequence was delayed beyond the point where survival was possible, indicating a performance/timing error during execution of the planned sequence.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / low pull
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The cause of death is listed as 'low pull' and possible factors include 'extracted pilot chute too late.' The jumper exited with a slightly head-low attitude (unstable exit) and failed to deploy the parachute at a survivable altitude. He was found wrapped in his canopy 60 metres from the base of the cliff, indicating the canopy was extracted but far too late for adequate inflation and deceleration. The execution failure was in not deploying the PC at the correct time despite presumably intending to do so.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose high-commitment proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Both jumpers chose to fly through 'the notch,' a line described as requiring near-perfect execution with approximately 200ft clearance over a ridge in the best case scenario, where the narrator states 'very few people in the world are capable of' the required exit and flight. The line offered minimal margin — even a perfect flight cleared the ridge by only ~200ft — and the narrator, a close peer, had explicitly told both jumpers the line 'wasn't worth it' because it offered no meaningful gain. This was a deliberate selection of a flight path with essentially zero margin for any deviation from perfection, constituting a decision that was identifiably and substantially inadequate given the known constraints.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Graham's sudden turn toward notch
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Graham made a 'sudden hard right towards the notch for unknown reasons,' passing in front of Dean and impacting the right side of the notch. The narrative explicitly states it is unclear whether this was an intentional course correction, a response to perceived inability to clear the upper ridge, or caused by an unknown weather condition. There is insufficient information to determine whether this was a decision error or an execution/control error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Dean's impact cause unclear
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Dean made it through the lower portion of the notch but 'likely impacted a tree' on the downhill side. It is unclear whether Dean was burbled by Graham's pass in front of him, was already too low, or experienced some other factor. The narrative states it is 'impossible to know' the specific mechanism, making it insufficient to classify as decision or performance error beyond the initial line selection.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose high-commitment proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Both jumpers chose to fly through 'the notch,' a line described as requiring near-perfect execution with approximately 200ft clearance over a ridge in the best case scenario. The narrator, a close associate, had explicitly told both jumpers the line 'wasn't worth it' and that it offered no meaningful benefit to the flight. Despite this, both committed to a line where the margin between survival and impact was extremely thin, and where even minor deviations in exit quality, glide performance, or unexpected interactions (such as burble from another jumper) left no room for recovery. The decision to fly this specific line was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome — both died from terminal impact into terrain features within the notch.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Graham's sudden turn toward notch
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Graham made a sudden hard right turn toward the notch for unknown reasons after initially setting up on the left side. The narrative explicitly states it is unclear whether this was intentional repositioning, a reaction to perceived inability to clear the upper ridge, or caused by an unknown weather condition. This maneuver placed him on a collision course with terrain and may have contributed to Dean's fatal outcome through burble or by forcing Dean into a compromised position, but insufficient information exists to classify the specific error type.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to deploy his pilot chute but failed on the first two pull attempts, only succeeding on the third attempt. By that point there was insufficient altitude remaining, and he impacted at line stretch. The repeated failed pulls represent a performance/execution error in the physical act of deploying the parachute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flying non-current wingsuit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly his old X2 wingsuit after not flying it for one year, despite being current on a different suit (Aura). This decision to fly a non-current suit on a terrain flight resulted in degraded performance — he was slow and went off-route, placing him in a position where he lacked adequate altitude and separation when he needed to deploy. The decision to use non-current equipment for a flight requiring precise performance was clearly and specifically inappropriate.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate wingsuit flight execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exit, the jumper did not begin effectively flying his wingsuit until approximately 100ft over the trees at the end of the talus, and the suit was not performing well throughout the approximately 30-second flight. He maintained minimal separation from terrain, consistent with flying too slowly or at an inefficient angle of attack, resulting in loss of speed and glide that left him unable to gain adequate separation from terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The pilot chute was found still inside the BOC after impact, indicating the jumper never successfully deployed his parachute. With insufficient terrain separation due to poor wingsuit performance, the jumper was unable to reach his BOC or chose not to attempt deployment, resulting in closed-container terminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of terrain impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was found approximately 2/3 through the normal wingsuit flight path with nothing deployed, having impacted terrain in terminal freefall. He was last seen flying in fine form around the first corner. There is no video evidence and the narrative explicitly states there is no basis for speculation about what occurred between the last sighting and impact. The cause of the fatal error — whether a decision regarding line choice, loss of speed/glide, spatial misjudgment, or other execution failure — cannot be determined from available information.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to cleanly extract and deploy the pilot chute from the BOC pouch during a wingsuit flight, resulting in a PC/bridle entanglement that rendered the PC unable to extract the canopy. The domain reference identifies that PC/bridle entanglement is caused by improperly deployed PCs (weak toss, holding PC too long allowing bridle to flow downstream) and that the PC must be deployed into clean air with clear, unwavering intent. The jumper's three subsequent attempts to extract the canopy by hand were unsuccessful before ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle entanglement during aerobatic deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed 3 backflips before deploying at approximately 4-5 seconds. During or after the aerobatic maneuver, the bridle entangled with his foot. Aerobatic deployments are recognized as creating high risk of bridle entanglement due to non-belly-to-earth body positions, and the domain reference explicitly identifies deploying head-down and experiencing bridle entanglement with a foot as both a total error and total malfunction. The entanglement was the direct mechanism that prevented canopy inflation, producing the fatal horseshoe configuration and closed-container impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit from a new exit point behind the normal exit on Gavea, where the rock drop was considerably short. Selecting an exit with insufficient vertical relief for wingsuit flight is a clearly and specifically wrong decision — a wingsuit requires adequate altitude to transition from exit to flying speed, and a 'considerably short' rock drop does not provide this. The jumper impacted the terrain seconds after exiting, indicating the chosen exit point was fundamentally inadequate for wingsuit flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Magnus was flying a proximity line ('the Barn Line') in a wingsuit and impacted terrain in terminal freefall. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall with 'loss of speed-glide' listed as a possible factor, indicating he failed to maintain adequate airspeed and glide performance during the flight to clear terrain. He was an experienced jumper who had chosen this line deliberately; the fatal outcome resulted from insufficient execution of the flight rather than from the decision to fly the line itself.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during terrain flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a proximity line along terrain when he lost adequate speed and glide performance, resulting in impact with terrain (through a small tree/bush) during wingsuit flight. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating he struck terrain before deploying his parachute. The narrative indicates loss of speed-glide as a possible factor, and the impact occurred on a technical part of the line, suggesting an execution failure in maintaining adequate airspeed and flight path rather than a fundamentally wrong decision about the line itself, which he had previously flown successfully.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate speed/glide management near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was observed flying lower and slower than the lead jumper during proximity flight along terrain. When encountering a ledge, the jumper lacked sufficient speed and glide performance to clear it, resulting in full-flight impact with the mountainside. Witnesses confirmed the jumper was 'not flying as well/fast' as the first jumper, indicating a failure in execution of speed and angle-of-attack management rather than a flawed plan, as the lead jumper successfully cleared the same terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a bad push from the exit point and failed to achieve sufficient forward separation, resulting in impact with a ledge only seconds after exit. The exit technique was insufficient for this technical exit which required precise execution to clear terrain features near the exit point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in extreme winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative explicitly states the jumper jumped in 'extremely high winds' that 'played a huge factor in this fatality.' The domain reference establishes that few objects are good in winds >10 mph and no objects are good >20 mph. The decision to exit in extremely high winds from a cliff in a wingsuit was clearly and specifically inappropriate — strong winds at a cliff exit are a recognized contraindication for jumping, and the conditions directly produced the unstable exit and inability to recover. This was not an execution failure; the jumper could not have been expected to fly successfully in these conditions regardless of skill.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed recovery from unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exiting, the jumper flipped and could not recover in time, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit. The jumper failed to regain a flyable body position from the unstable attitude induced by the wind conditions. However, this execution failure was substantially a product of the decision to jump in extreme winds.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate wingsuit familiarity for BASE
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used a wingsuit he had only skydived twice before attempting a wingsuit BASE jump from a technical exit. Accepted practice requires 40-60 skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE use. This decision was clearly and specifically wrong — jumping a near-unfamiliar wingsuit from a technical cliff exit meant the jumper lacked the suit-specific knowledge needed to manage the start arc, and this inadequacy directly produced the failed start and subterminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed wingsuit start / unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to achieve adequate flight after exit, resulting in a 'fail to a short start' with subterminal impact and nothing deployed. The execution failure — whether an unstable exit, incorrect angle of attack, or inability to transition to flight in the unfamiliar suit — was the direct mechanism producing the fatal ground impact with no canopy deployed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit and failed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reportedly slipped on exit and was unable to establish stable wingsuit flight. He was observed falling straight down with only his pilot chute out, impacting on the talus near the wall beneath the exit point. The unstable exit and failure to recover into stable flight was the execution error that produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Stall during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Philippe Jean flew his wingsuit toward a challenging entrance point on line 7, reached to make the point, stalled the wingsuit, and lost flight, resulting in terminal impact with the grassy slope. The recovered camera video confirms he stalled while attempting to access the entrance, indicating a technique/execution failure in airspeed and angle of attack management during the critical phase of flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate landing area selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumpers chose a very small landing area closer to the ocean to avoid crossing a stream, in conditions of steady tailwind and violent winter swell at near high tide, which reduced the already small LZ further. This decision to use an undersized landing area in poor conditions with inadequate margin from the ocean was the producing mechanism: the tailwind reduced canopy penetration, and the jumper landed in the wave wash, leading to drowning. The narrative explicitly states conditions were poor, the LZ was smaller than usual, and the object is rated as highly technical.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper collapsed his leg wing before initiating the pull, causing excess fabric to cover the BOC/PC handle. He was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute despite several seconds of attempting, resulting in a total malfunction (closed container impact). The intended action was to deploy the parachute, but the execution technique—collapsing wings before reaching the PC handle—prevented successful extraction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in inadequate weather
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited in bad weather with low-level fog and medium winds, which the police report states could have increased the chance of impact from low visibility. Despite being highly experienced with 1400 WS BASE jumps and intimate familiarity with this exit point, the decision to jump in conditions with low-level clouds and fog was clearly inappropriate as it degraded or eliminated the visual references essential for wingsuit terrain flying. The result was terminal freefall impact, consistent with inability to see and avoid terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping antenna in excessive winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump a guyed antenna at night with winds of 31 knots at 1600 feet and 20 knots at the midpoint. The domain reference states few objects are good in winds over 10 mph and no objects are good over 20 mph. Guyed antennas restrict desirable heading range to approximately 120 degrees, and wind evaluation is mandatory because wind conditions frequently rule out safe jump plans. Jumping a guyed antenna in 31-knot winds (approximately 36 mph) at exit altitude is a clearly and specifically inappropriate decision that produced the fatal object strike under canopy, as crosswinds degraded heading performance and canopy control near guy wires.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed his pilot chute too late, at an altitude insufficient for the canopy to fully inflate before ground impact. The narrative states he was 'going faster and lower than the rest of the three way' and 'started to pull over the road or very close to it' when he was already critically low. The canopy inflated right before impact, confirming the deployment was initiated far too late. This represents a failure in altitude awareness and timely execution of deployment rather than a flawed decision about the jump plan itself — the jumper intended to deploy but failed to do so at a survivable altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear cause of total malfunction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a total malfunction with only the pilot chute deploying (PC in tow). Investigators found a hole in the PC netting and a broken zipper on the right side of the harness, with a witness stating the bridle appeared caught on something. However, the narrative does not establish clearly whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., choosing to jump with damaged equipment) or a performance error (e.g., packing or rigging error that caused the bridle to snag). The relationship between the hole in the PC, the broken zipper, and the bridle entanglement is not sufficiently explained to distinguish between decision and execution failure.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper used a two-move dynamic exit with his right foot advanced, and during the push he likely stepped on his leg wing and lost grip with his right foot, causing him to slip. This resulted in an immediate 45-degree bank to the left within less than a second, escalating to a 90-degree bank with the jumper struggling not to turn on his back. Despite recovering from the near-inversion and completing a roughly 270-degree turn, he was unable to establish a viable flight path and impacted terrain approximately 200 meters below the exit point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in gusty wind conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper initially waited approximately two hours because conditions were too windy, then declined to jump with his partner specifically because winds had become gusty. After waiting only 15 more minutes he decided to exit solo in conditions that had not meaningfully improved — winds were still visible on the grass at the exit and described as moderate to gusty. Jumping a large wingsuit (Vampire Race) in gusty crosswind conditions from this object was a clearly inappropriate decision that directly contributed to the fatal outcome by degrading exit stability and eliminating any possibility of recovery from the resulting unstable exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide during terrain flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was performing a wingsuit proximity flight through a gulley near the rifugio. Evidence indicates he lost speed and glide performance during the flight, as he impacted terrain at high speed in terminal freefall with only an emergency-level low deployment attempt (slider still at top of lines). His flight path deviated from the original plan—turning south and entering a gulley east of the rifugio where he disappeared from view—suggesting he was unable to maintain adequate energy and separation from terrain. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, consistent with a wingsuit pilot who lost available lift and could not recover or deploy in time.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle misrouting during field repack
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After discovering a dislodged bottom pin at the exit point, the jumper re-closed the container without removing the bridle and PC from their stow. This resulted in the bridle being routed inside the closing loop, creating a pin lock malfunction. The PC deployed and extracted fully but could not pull the pins, resulting in a total malfunction and high-speed impact. This was an execution error in a task the jumper knew how to perform — the correct procedure (removing the bridle/PC before re-closing) was skipped during the field adjustment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Slow/stalled wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Gary exited and immediately flew with very little forward speed, consistent with a stalled or near-stalled wingsuit configuration. His forward speed continued to decrease as he attempted to fly over the power lines, indicating he was flying at too high an angle of attack and failing to achieve efficient wingsuit flight. This poor execution consumed all available altitude, leaving him with insufficient altitude to complete deployment after clearing the power lines.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped beyond demonstrated ability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative establishes that Gary's previous jump from the same object the day before resulted in a stalled flight requiring an emergency pitch and an unplanned highway landing. Despite receiving extensive coaching about his deficiencies (exit technique, angle of attack, flight in non-thermic conditions), he chose to jump the same object again the next morning under similar shaded/non-thermic conditions. The prior day's near-miss clearly demonstrated that this object exceeded his wingsuit capability, making the decision to repeat the jump identifiably and specifically wrong given the information available to him.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit angle execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: JVH exited on the right position but pushed to the same axis as the first jumper rather than maintaining a different angle. The pre-jump discussion established that the right-side jumper should push parallel or at a slightly different angle, but JVH flew into the first jumper's flight path, entering his burble approximately 4 seconds into the jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted fly-out instead of deploying
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After hitting the burble and experiencing clear loss of power at approximately 4 seconds, JVH chose to attempt to fly it out by turning 90 degrees right at a nose-up pitch rather than deploying his parachute while he still had altitude. He continued in degraded flight for another 3-4 seconds in a nose-up, energy-depleting configuration until impact at 10 seconds. The nose-up turn bled remaining speed and available lift, and the decision to continue flying rather than deploy produced the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After initiating the left turn over the ridgeline, Jimmy progressively pulled the suit to a shallower angle of attack in an attempt to stay above terrain rather than turning out toward safer ground. This slow-flight technique bled airspeed and available lift, causing severe lateral instability and an unrecoverable sink rate. The execution error was in flying too slowly near terrain — a recognized fatal pattern — rather than maintaining speed or converting to a dive to regain energy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew unfamiliar line with minimal suit experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jimmy chose to fly a turning line over ridges requiring terrain management, despite having only 2 skydives and approximately 13 BASE jumps on the V3 (a suit he was unfamiliar with, purchased as a gap-filler), and despite his previous Brento jumps being described as 'not very good, rather unsteady.' The decision to fly a committing terrain line — rather than going straight out as he had done successfully — was clearly inappropriate given his demonstrated lack of proficiency on this suit. He had a safe straight-out option and an alternative landing area available.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Rigging error bridle-canopy separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The bridle separated from the canopy on deployment, indicating the bridle was not properly attached to the canopy during rigging/inspection. This is a memory or technique failure in executing a known critical rigging step — the jumper intended to have a properly configured system but failed in the physical execution of ensuring the bridle-canopy connection. The narrative states 'very likely a rigging error' and that the bridle separated from the canopy on opening, resulting in a total malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose object requiring mandatory perfection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump an exit known to be extremely difficult, featuring a downhill exit requiring many steps with a ledge at 300' that previous jumpers had barely cleared by inches — one of whom vowed never to jump it again. The decision to attempt this object, which demanded mandatory perfection with essentially zero margin for error at the ledge, was the producing mechanism: the jumper did not clear the ledge and impacted near the exit. The object's known characteristics made it a clear case where any less-than-perfect execution would be fatal, and the decision to jump it was identifiably and specifically inadequate.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed deployment cause unknown
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall with nothing out, indicating a failure to deploy the parachute. The narrative states he was having trouble flying a borrowed tracking suit, but provides no specific detail about what prevented deployment — whether it was a decision to delay too long, inability to reach the BOC due to suit problems, loss of altitude awareness, or some other factor. There is insufficient information to distinguish between a decision error and a performance error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committing line in degraded conditions
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly the most committing entrance to line 3 — where the ridge is flat and bail-out requires a sharp 90-110 degree turn — during late afternoon when thermal lift had diminished compared to his earlier jumps. He had observed himself 10-15 meters above the ridge on previous jumps but those were made with more lift available. The decision to fly this committing line in conditions with less lift than previously experienced was the producing mechanism: he lacked the margin to clear the ridge and the line geometry prevented escape once he recognized the problem.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient speed management
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper sank out and impacted the ridge, indicating he did not maintain sufficient airspeed to achieve the glide needed to clear the terrain. The narrative identifies loss of speed-glide as a possible factor, and the lessons emphasize that speed was lost while trying to maintain glide. Inadequate speed management — failing to maintain the energy reserves needed to convert to glide at the critical ridge crossing — was the execution-level failure that directly produced the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient object separation / tracking performance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited cleanly and established a steep track, but failed to achieve sufficient glide to clear a known terrain feature (the first notch) that trackers must out-fly on this line. She impacted the top of the notch in terminal freefall without deploying her parachute, indicating she either did not recognize the terrain closure in time or was unable to generate the glide performance needed. The narrative describes a 'low flight pattern' and an attempt to 'out track the first notch' that was unsuccessful, pointing to inadequate tracking execution or misjudgment of glide performance relative to terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit / slip
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reportedly slipped on snow at the exit point, resulting in an unstable exit from which he was unable to recover. The cause of death was subterminal impact near the exit, indicating he never achieved a stable body position or adequate separation to deploy his parachute. The execution failure (slipping) produced the fatal outcome directly.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and his legs went over his back, causing an unstable exit that prevented him from establishing flight. This execution failure directly resulted in him failing to clear the ledge below the exit point, leading to subterminal impact within seconds of exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose line with insufficient clearance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper elected to fly a left line that took him over a ridge he did not have the height or glide to clear. This was a conscious line selection that was clearly and specifically inadequate for the terrain, as the chosen trajectory placed him on a collision course with a ridge without sufficient altitude or glide performance to pass it. The decision to fly that line was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to deploy emergency parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative states the jumper 'failed to make an emergency pull' after misjudging his position relative to the ridge. Once the line choice proved unviable, deploying the parachute was the remaining survival action available, and the failure to execute this pull resulted in impact in terminal freefall with no canopy deployed.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose jump requiring zero margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a 'very positive' proximity tracking line at Chapeau de Napoléon that the narrative explicitly describes as 'lacking any sort of margin for human or environmental suboptimal parameters.' This was a decision to commit to a line where any deviation from perfect performance or conditions would be fatal, constituting a course of action that was clearly and specifically inadequate given the zero-margin nature of the terrain. He clipped a ledge during the proximity flight and impacted in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Aggressive maneuver execution error
