L1_unsafe_acts.description: Uli attempted a radical 90-degree right turn toward the base flyer to close the altitude gap, but misjudged his closing speed. The maneuver was executed too aggressively, and when he braced for the resulting near-collision by closing his right arm wing and bending his right leg, this distortion put the suit into a hard diving right turn toward the wall from which he could not recover. The narrative explicitly states he 'executed the maneuver too aggressively' and 'appeared to misjudge his closing speed,' indicating a technique/execution failure rather than a fundamentally wrong decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with object strike
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed an exit that was 'too head low and not far away,' resulting in insufficient separation from the cliff. Approximately 2 seconds after exit, he clipped a ledge with his feet, which threw him into a head-down position from which he never recovered, impacting the terrain approximately 7-8 seconds later with no canopy deployed. The exit technique failure was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error during aerobatic maneuver
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was performing an aerobatic maneuver (barrel roll or corkscrew roll) during wingsuit proximity flight and impacted trees during the maneuver. The jumper intended to execute the maneuver and continue flying but failed in execution, resulting in terrain impact at terminal freefall speed. The fatal outcome was produced by insufficient technique or spatial awareness during the maneuver rather than by the decision to attempt it, as the maneuver could theoretically be survived with correct execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The 250-meter wall had a ledge just below the exit requiring a running exit or very strong push to clear. The jumper clipped the wall with his feet shortly after exiting, indicating insufficient push/run to achieve the separation needed to clear the ledge. This inadequate exit execution directly produced the object strike that caused the fatal injuries.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity flight beyond margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall during a wingsuit proximity flight, with loss of speed-glide identified as a possible factor. Flying a proximity line that left insufficient margin to recover from speed/glide degradation represents a decision that was clearly inappropriate — the chosen flight path did not allow survival when available lift diminished. The decision to fly close enough to terrain that a loss of speed-glide resulted in unrecoverable impact is the producing mechanism.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited 45 degrees head high, resulting in an upright/knee-first falling position rather than achieving forward flight. This poor exit technique prevented adequate forward separation and created a steep recovery arc that was insufficient to clear the ledge 150 meters below the exit point, resulting in chest impact with the ledge.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to maintain adequate airspeed
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to monitor and maintain adequate airspeed during wingsuit flight. After achieving a good start, he transitioned to maximum glide at approximately 100m above terrain without recognizing progressive speed loss. By the time the suit began to stall, he lacked sufficient altitude and positioning to recover. The eyewitness explicitly states 'He lost his speed slowly and didn't realise he was losing speed' and 'He had not enough experience with this suit to realise he was losing speed,' indicating an attention/monitoring failure during execution rather than a deliberate decision to fly slowly.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose unviable proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a flight line that required flying over the original Melchstul exit ledge at extremely close proximity. Impact occurred just after crossing the ledge, indicating the chosen line required a level of terrain clearance performance that left zero margin. This was a deliberate line selection that was clearly and specifically inadequate — the jumper committed to flying over/past terrain with insufficient altitude and energy reserves to clear it, resulting in impact in terminal freefall with no opportunity to deploy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumping in excessive wind
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited in winds of 5-10 m/s (11-22 mph), which is at or beyond the upper limit for safe BASE jumping and particularly hazardous for wingsuit exits where stable flight initiation is critical. The domain reference states few objects are good above 10 mph and none above 20 mph. The strong winds were a known condition at the time of the decision to jump, and the decision to exit in these conditions produced the unstable/tumbling flight from which the jumper never recovered, resulting in subterminal impact with no parachute deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a bad exit from an unforgiving exit point and impacted terrain a few seconds later. The narrative and possible factors indicate an unstable exit that failed to achieve adequate separation from the object, resulting in subterminal impact near the exit. The execution of the exit was the producing mechanism — a properly executed exit from this object could have been survivable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper initiated the correct pull maneuver but was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC. Rather than returning to stable flight to reattempt, he continued searching while losing speed, altitude, and stability, eventually becoming unstable and rolling until ground impact. This was a technique/execution error in the deployment sequence - the correct action was intended but physical execution failed, resulting in inability to deploy the parachute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit leading to loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed a noticeably head-low exit from Pilastro. This unstable exit in crosswind conditions resulted in a corkscrew (uncontrolled rotation) from which the jumper was unable to recover, and the wingsuit flew him into the wall approximately 4 seconds after exit. The exit technique failure was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed off-heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper received a 180-degree off-heading opening on a slider-down cliff jump. Witness accounts and gear inspection confirm that toggles were never unstowed, indicating the jumper failed to execute an effective heading correction using rear risers or brakes. The canopy flew back into the cliff, striking it multiple times before the jumper came to rest on the talus, dying from impact injuries.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient speed margin for terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a full-glide proximity line above a ridge that required at least 3.5 glide ratio, without maintaining adequate speed reserves. His brother noted he 'did not leave enough safety margin' and that 'it's never a good idea to proximity fly without some speed in reserve.' The decision to commit to this line without retaining sufficient energy to manage the flattening ridge profile and potential atmospheric changes (descending cooler air) was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed speed/energy management during flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: At the end of the ridge where terrain flattened and required higher glide performance, the jumper had insufficient speed and height. When he turned toward the landing zone, he encountered descending air and lost flight characteristics rapidly. His inability to maintain adequate airspeed through the critical section — arriving at the end of the ridge without enough energy to either clear terrain or execute the turn — represents a failure in execution of speed and energy management during the flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and deploy pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate and extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket during his wingsuit deployment attempt. Witnesses observed him struggling with multiple different moves to find the PC, including de-arching instead of arching, and ultimately turning onto his back before impact. The pilot chute was found just out of the pocket with the bridle still in folds post-impact, confirming it was never successfully deployed during flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose proximity line exceeding margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a 'rather left flight path line' — one of the more challenging lines — that required flying over a ridge with trees. Given the available direct/easy line to the landing area, the jumper's choice to fly a proximity line over terrain he needed to clear, on his second jump of the day from this exit, with relatively few jumps on this wingsuit (10-12), constitutes a decision that was clearly and specifically inadequate. The decision to fly this line placed him in a no-pull zone over a ridge he failed to clear, resulting in terminal impact with no deployment attempt.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued flight without deploying
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David flew a slow, low flight path over the plateau and was observed to have had multiple opportunities to perform an emergency low pull. Rather than deploying his parachute when it became clear he lacked adequate altitude and speed to complete the planned line, he decided to try to pass the plateau and impacted approximately 2m before its end without having attempted to pull. The decision to continue flying through a no-pull zone rather than deploying when opportunities existed was the producing mechanism of the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: PC deployment through compression cord
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reached his hand through the stash bag compression cord and pulled the pilot chute out through it, causing the PC to become entangled with the compression cord. This execution error during the deployment sequence resulted in a PC in tow / PC-bridle entanglement and a total malfunction. The jumper intended to deploy but failed to execute the BOC reach correctly, routing the PC through an obstruction rather than cleanly extracting it.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature deployment while unstable
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deployed at approximately 3-4 seconds into a jump configured for an 8-9 second delay (slider-up), while in an unstable body position after failing to establish a controlled track. This premature, unstable deployment at the vertical section of the cliff produced a violent ~140° off-heading opening with canopy surge, resulting in fatal object strike. The slider-up configuration at sub-terminal airspeed degraded heading performance, and the unstable body position further compromised the opening, but the core failure was the execution: deploying at the wrong time, in the wrong body position, at the wrong altitude relative to the cliff profile.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had a bad exit, possibly slipping on the wet/damp exit point, resulting in a head-down attitude from which he could not recover. He impacted a ledge approximately 5 seconds after exit with no canopy deployment, indicating the unstable exit directly produced the fatal conditions by leaving him head-down without sufficient time or separation to recover and deploy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committed to no-out line without adequate margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued into a known no-pull zone with no out to the right despite having lower groundspeed and a higher-than-normal glide ratio (indicating near-maximum AoA with minimal energy reserves). The pre-jump plan explicitly called for peeling out to the right before the no-out section if the terrain was not flyable, yet the jumper committed to the line rather than executing the planned escape. This decision to continue into terrain with no escape route while already flying at marginal energy states was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to maintain adequate airspeed
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew at a high glide ratio with insufficient airspeed, consistent with flying too slowly near stall. After encountering the burble from Jumper 2, the already marginal speed was further reduced, leading to a wingsuit stall with no altitude or energy to recover. The domain reference identifies flying at high AoA near stall as a recognized fatal error pattern in wingsuit BASE, and the jumper's groundspeed was already lower than on previous successful flights of the same line.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Airspeed management failure during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During wingsuit proximity flight, the jumper lost speed mid-flight, which caused altitude loss and placed her close to terrain. The domain reference establishes that speed loss in a wingsuit rapidly depletes available lift (a 20% speed decrease results in 36% AL loss), and that flying too slowly is a recognized execution failure pattern where the pilot allows AoA to increase, bleeds energy, and enters a degraded flight state. The jumper was unable to recover sufficient energy to clear the terrain, resulting in impact in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit with failed recovery
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited head-down from a wingsuit exit at 6000m with only 85m to a ledge below. He was unable to recover from the head-low exit attitude quickly enough to establish forward flight, resulting in impact with the ledge 85m below the exit point. The exit execution was the producing mechanism — a stable, properly executed wingsuit exit could have allowed forward flight away from the terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unable to locate/extract pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Kristian attempted to pull for 5-6 seconds but was unable to locate or extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket. The narrative indicates he had lost feeling in his fingers and hands due to cold exposure, resulting in inability to physically execute the pull despite repeated attempts. He impacted without deploying his parachute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and deploy PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: GoPro footage clearly shows a series of attempts to find and deploy the pilot chute, all of which failed. The jumper intended to deploy but was unable to locate the BOC handle, resulting in no parachute deployment and closed-container impact. This is an execution failure in a fundamental skill — extracting the pilot chute from the BOC pouch during wingsuit flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unpulled wingsuit impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted subterminal near the exit without deploying the parachute. The narrative states a possible unstable exit and strong winds, but the jumper was found the next day with no witnesses, so it cannot be determined whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., choosing to jump in strong winds on a very committing line) or a performance error (e.g., unstable exit leading to loss of control and inability to deploy). Insufficient information exists to discriminate between decision and execution failure.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate stowage of climbing sling
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper stowed his climbing sling and non-locking carabiner by tucking them into the saddlebag opening on his leg strap. During climbing and sitting on the railing, the right-side carabiner worked halfway out of its stowage. This inadequate stowage/failure to verify secure stowage before exit allowed the bridle to lay over the carabiner gate, which then clipped the bridle when tension was applied, choking off the canopy and producing a total malfunction resulting in impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Aerial from inadequate object/experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to perform a gainer from a slider-up antenna with only 2-3 prior slider-up jumps and no established competence in aerials (described as performing 'poor gainers' that were 'rather unstable and uncontrolled'). Attempting an aerial from his first slider-up object with minimal slider-up experience and demonstrated inability to execute gainers competently was a clearly inappropriate decision. The domain reference states aerials exponentially increase danger and require careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed gainer execution and late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper could not set the rotation, hovered, rolled over his side, then attempted to stabilize and turn away from the antenna. He deployed his PC too late, impacting without a canopy. The failed aerial execution consumed altitude and the delayed deployment was the direct mechanism producing the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate pilot chute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted to deploy at a reasonably high altitude but was unable to locate the pilot chute in the BOC pocket. Video evidence shows the jumper made a pull attempt but could not find the PC, then flipped over and impacted the ground. This is an execution failure during a correctly intended action — the jumper knew to pull and attempted to do so but failed in the physical execution of locating and extracting the pilot chute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Slow flight into terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Oscar flew the same proximity line as his morning jump but lower and slower, without adequate speed/energy. The video shows slower-than-normal flight for at least the last 10 seconds, during which he appeared fixated on buzzing the trees on the ridge shoulder. In the final second he lost his flight, was unable to clear the trees, and it was too late to turn right to the available escape route. This constitutes a failure to maintain adequate airspeed and available lift — a fundamental execution requirement for proximity wingsuit flying that he understood but failed to perform on this flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper clipped the underhung section of the cliff with his legs immediately upon exiting, indicating an insufficient exit push or improper body position that failed to clear the terrain. This contact sent him into a violent spin from which he was unable to recover before impact. The exit execution failure was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment / altitude awareness failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to deploy her pilot chute at a survivable altitude. Despite being described as a habitual 'high puller,' she extracted the pilot chute too late, resulting in only approximately 2 seconds under canopy before impact. The narrative indicates she likely lost altitude awareness, possibly due to visual fixation on the landing area rather than monitoring her proximity to terrain ('my guess is that her visuals were at the landing and that she did not see the ledge coming'). This was an attention/monitoring failure during execution rather than a decision error — the jump plan itself (terminal tracking, slider-up) was appropriate for the object, but the execution of altitude monitoring and timely deployment was insufficient.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late aggressive maneuvering toward terrain gate
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After initially flying at an oblique angle away from the tree gate, Alex made a late decision to bank hard right and then left to realign on the gate. This late course correction at approximately 8 seconds into the flight caused significant speed and altitude loss, placing him in a critically high AoA from which he could not recover. The decision to aggressively maneuver back toward the terrain feature rather than continuing the safer detour around it to the left was the producing mechanism for the fatal stall and impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed sub-terminal tracking execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and swept into a tracking position but immediately began turning to the right, achieving almost no forward separation from the wall. This is a technique/execution failure in sub-terminal tracking — the jumper intended to track away from the cliff but failed to maintain heading and generate adequate horizontal distance. The narrative notes he 'had a tendency to turn once he was up to speed,' indicating a recurring execution deficiency rather than a deliberate decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment with insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: At 10 seconds the jumper pitched his pilot chute while facing the wall with no meaningful object separation. Having failed to achieve forward distance, deploying at that point without adequate separation was a failure to recognize and respond to the critical situation — he needed to deploy earlier while still facing away from the wall or at a point where separation existed. The deployment timing and orientation constituted an attention/awareness failure during execution, resulting in impact at approximately line stretch.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Weak exit with poor body position
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Rob performed a weak push from the exit point combined with a head-high posture. This execution failure resulted in insufficient forward separation to clear a significant positive incline approximately 6-8m below the exit, causing his left foot to strike the cliff and initiating a fatal tumble. The exit technique — not the decision to jump — was the producing mechanism; with a stronger push and proper body position, the ledge could have been cleared.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wrong exit point selected
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited from a position that did not provide adequate clearance over a large ledge 30 meters below. The narrative explicitly states this was 'a case of jumping from the wrong exit point.' The other jumper, positioned roughly a meter to the left, barely cleared the ledge, while Bjørn Emil's exit position on the right side — where the ledge gradually protruded further — made clearance impossible. The decision to exit from that specific point, given the terrain geometry, was the producing mechanism of the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of altitude awareness
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After performing a multi-person aerial exit (totem pole front flip), the jumper broke off facing the cliff, rotated 180 degrees to face away, and deployed very low. The canopy extracted but did not fully inflate before impact. The narrative indicates the jumper lost altitude awareness while focusing on turning away from the cliff, resulting in a fatally late deployment. The aerials were intentional and within the jumper's chosen plan; the fatal mechanism was the failure to monitor altitude and deploy in time.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle entanglement during aerial deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a gainer (aerial) and became entangled with the bridle upon pulling. The domain reference explicitly identifies bridle entanglement as a known risk of aerials, where deploying from non-belly-to-earth positions risks the bridle wrapping around limbs or torso. Although the jumper cleared the entanglement, the delay consumed the remaining altitude, resulting in impact at line stretch. The fatal mechanism was the execution failure during deployment from the aerial maneuver, not the decision to perform a gainer itself.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wingsuit BASE with no training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a wingsuit BASE jump with only 40 skydives, 1 BASE jump (this fatal one), no BASE training, no mentorship, and no First Jump Course, using skydiving gear (Micron container with skydiving main/reserve and deployment bag) rather than BASE-specific equipment. Accepted progression requires 200+ skydives before beginning BASE, 100+ BASE jumps before wingsuit BASE, and 100+ wingsuit skydives, plus proper BASE equipment. The decision to attempt this jump was a clear and extreme departure from accepted practice, and the use of skydiving gear (which lacks the rapid deployment characteristics of BASE equipment and includes a deployment bag unsuitable for BASE) was identified as the cause of death. The canopy was observed slowly turning back into the wall, consistent with inadequate equipment and complete lack of training producing an unrecoverable situation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper suddenly dove 45 degrees to the left and down, flipped onto his back, and impacted trees in terminal freefall with no parachute deployment. The witness observed a 'twitch' in flight immediately prior. The wingsuit was inspected post-accident and showed no damage that could have caused the event. A possible medical explanation (seizure history) exists but remains speculative; the narrative does not provide sufficient information to definitively determine whether the fatal loss of control resulted from a medical incapacitation, a decision error, or a performance error.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity line through terrain gate
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After a normal exit and four seconds of straight flight, the jumper deliberately banked left to fly through a narrow 'tree gate' formed by a lone tree on a pillar and the wall. This proximity maneuver through a constricted terrain feature on his first jump from this exit required precise speed and altitude management with minimal margin for error. The turn caused him to lose speed and altitude, and he failed to clear the pillar by a narrow margin, resulting in high-speed terrain impact. The decision to fly a proximity line through a tight terrain gate on a first flight from an unfamiliar exit was clearly and specifically inappropriate — the domain reference establishes that first flights from new lines should maintain distance from terrain, and flying between terrain features 'greatly reduces available solutions.'

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable aerial over-rotation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a double front flip on exit which over-rotated, leaving him unstable and close to the wall. The over-rotation was an execution error in the aerial maneuver — the intended action (front flips) was performed with insufficient technique, resulting in loss of stability and inadequate object separation. This directly produced the fatal conditions: proximity to the wall with insufficient altitude or separation to recover, leading to an emergency pull and wall impact at terminal velocity.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of glide leading to terrain impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit proximity line requiring very good glide and early commitment. He lost sufficient speed/glide to clear the terrain, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. The evidence that he pulled his parachute indicates he recognized the emergency but was unable to recover from the situation, pointing to a failure in execution of the flight (maintaining adequate speed and glide) rather than a fundamentally wrong decision about the line itself.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Lluis performed a few-steps exit that resulted in a very head-low and asymmetric body position. This unstable exit failed to generate adequate forward separation from the cliff, leading to impact with a ledge directly below the exit point at subterminal speed. The execution of the exit was the producing mechanism: a properly executed exit from this object could have provided sufficient separation to survive.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of wall impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted the wall/ledges near the exit point, with possible factors including a slipped/bad exit or poor tracking. The camera was lost so the exact mechanism is unknown. The narrative states 'bad exit / slipping on exit or just a bad track seem to be the likeliest' but cannot confirm which occurred, making it impossible to distinguish between a decision error (choosing this demanding exit in these conditions) and a performance error (execution failure during exit or track).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity line beyond margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a tight proximity line through a gorge with tight corners on what was most likely his first flight at Stockhorn, declining a 2-way with an experienced terrain flyer because that flyer wouldn't be 'charging hard enough.' The narrative establishes a consistent pattern of deliberately flying with insufficient margin (described as '110%'), and on this flight he was 'turning and possibly drifting too deep for that whole section, especially for a 1st flight there.' The decision to fly an aggressive, low-margin proximity line through unfamiliar terrain with tight corners was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew line requiring mandatory terrain clearance without margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to dive aggressively toward terrain at the bottom of the cliff on his first time at this object, committing to a line that required a successful flare to clear terrain with no margin for error. This constitutes flying into a no-pull zone / terrain proximity situation where the decision itself — choosing a line that demanded mandatory perfection to survive — was the producing mechanism. The domain reference explicitly warns against putting oneself 'behind something' where one 'needs' to increase glide to avoid an obstacle, and states that flying between/over terrain 'greatly reduces available solutions.' Flying an unfamiliar line with aggressive proximity on a first visit to the site is a clear departure from accepted practice, which requires knowing the line intimately, flying conservatively on first flights, and keeping distance that is gradually reduced over several sessions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC deployment technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The pilot chute slipped from the jumper's hand during the throw, causing it to fall directly behind his head/right shoulder region where it remained in the burble on top of his rig until impact. Video evidence clearly shows the PC was not deployed into clean air with sufficient force, resulting in a total malfunction (PC in tow). This is a technique/execution error in the physical act of deploying the pilot chute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited in a totally head-down orientation, failing to achieve a flyable body position. This unstable exit prevented effective wingsuit flight, resulting in insufficient separation from the cliff and multiple impacts with the wall near the exit point before achieving canopy deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted BASE with skydive gear and no training
L1_unsafe_acts.description: A 15-year-old with no instruction or training obtained skydiving gear and attempted a BASE jump from a building using a static line configuration. Skydiving equipment is not designed for BASE jumping — it lacks the rapid deployment characteristics, appropriate brake settings, and configuration necessary for the low-altitude, fixed-object environment. The decision to attempt a BASE jump with no training and inappropriate equipment was the producing mechanism for the fatality; even perfect execution could not have overcome the fundamental inadequacy of using unconfigured skydive gear from a building.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient deployment altitude
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deliberately delayed deployment to approximately 80 meters AGL, a critically insufficient altitude for a slider-up cliff jump. He had a documented pattern of opening lower than he should, having been warned by his mentor and other jumpers about this behavior. This decision left zero margin for any deployment difficulty, and when a PC extraction problem occurred, there was no altitude remaining to recover.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed PC extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: At the already critically low deployment altitude, the jumper failed to cleanly extract and deploy the pilot chute on the first attempt. Witnesses observed an attempt to open at approximately 80 meters, but the jumper could not complete deployment - possibly missing the PC handle, hand slipping off, or finger catching in the open PC handle. The jumper impacted before line stretch.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to locate and deploy PC
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was unable to locate his pilot chute at pull time, became unstable while searching for it, and impacted the ground without deploying his parachute. The narrative states he 'had issues at pull time' and was 'unstable looking for the PC,' indicating a failure in the physical execution of the deployment sequence — specifically the BOC reach and extraction — rather than a decision error. This execution failure directly produced the fatal total malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Altitude awareness failure during unzipping
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After deployment, Marcus flew away from the planned landing area (the road) while unzipping his wingsuit and performing a 180-degree turn. Upon completing the turn back toward the road, he was too low to reach it. This represents an attention/awareness failure — he failed to monitor his altitude and position relative to the landing area during the post-opening unzipping and maneuvering sequence, consuming too much altitude flying away from the only viable landing zone before turning back.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of flight impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted terrain in terminal freefall during a wingsuit proximity flight with his parachute container still closed and pilot chute stowed. The camera that might have revealed the cause was lost on impact. Without footage or witnesses to the final phase of flight, it is impossible to determine whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., choosing a line with insufficient margin) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., misjudging speed/glide, failing to maintain adequate terrain separation, or failing to deploy). The narrative establishes proximity flying as a possible factor and notes thermal/bumpy air conditions, but the specific mechanism that led to controlled flight into terrain cannot be determined from available evidence.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in strong winds at wrong time
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper acknowledged winds seemed strong, yet proceeded to jump during the warmer part of the day when coastal winds are known to be stronger, rather than the early morning window normally used for this site. This decision to jump in recognized adverse wind conditions at a suboptimal time was the producing mechanism: strong winds at a slider-down cliff with only ~4 seconds of usable delay likely caused or compounded an unstable exit and/or insufficient object separation, resulting in terminal freefall impact with the cliff.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unclear execution failure mechanism
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative lists multiple possible scenarios (unstable exit to impact, excessive delay, off-heading opening near wall) but cannot confirm which execution error occurred. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall with canopy out, suggesting a deployment or tracking execution failure, but insufficient detail exists to distinguish whether the jumper failed to deploy in time, had an unstable exit preventing effective tracking, or suffered wind-induced loss of separation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed pilot chute extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jon initiated deployment at a safe altitude but was unable to locate and/or extract the pilot chute from the BOC pocket despite multiple attempts. He struggled with the extraction throughout the remaining altitude, only deploying the PC at approximately 30-40 feet before impact, resulting in terminal impact with no effective canopy deployment. The failure was in the physical execution of the pull sequence rather than in the decision of when or whether to deploy.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Misrouting bridle through container
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper routed the bridle through the lateral band (a harness/container webbing) instead of the correct path. This misrigging meant that when the static line extracted the bridle, it could not extract the canopy from the container, resulting in a total malfunction. The jumper intended to rig the system correctly but made an execution error during the rigging process.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate malfunction response
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a tension knot causing a rapid spiral to the left. The jumper attempted rear riser pumping and brake toggle inputs without effect, briefly arrested the rotation with a flare but was unable to sustain controlled flight or execute an effective emergency response (such as cutting the affected line with a hook knife) before striking the talus. The malfunction response was insufficient to prevent object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit causing rotation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Ground crew observed that Dylan's poised exit was unstable and he rotated forward after exit. He pitched (deployed) mid-rotation, resulting in an off-heading opening with line twists. The unstable exit and deployment during rotation directly produced the off-heading that led to object strike and fatal injuries. The execution of the exit was the producing mechanism — a stable exit and properly timed deployment would have been survivable.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Eric appears to have had an unstable exit, likely slipping on wet grass, which resulted in his feet going over his head into a front flip/scorpion position. He impacted the first ledge approximately 5 seconds after exit only 8-12 feet from the wall, with his canopy only half out and one pin still in and PC still stowed, indicating he never achieved a stable body position or effective deployment. The execution failure on exit directly produced the fatal outcome by preventing adequate object separation and any possibility of deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed pilot chute deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Brandon made his initial attempt to deploy the pilot chute at approximately 4 seconds but failed to successfully extract and throw it. The video shows his hand reaching the BOC and arm/shoulder motion in an attempt to throw, but the deployment did not succeed — whether from a hard pull, missed pull, or fumble is unclear. This failed first attempt consumed approximately 1.1 seconds of critical altitude, and while he successfully deployed on his second attempt at 5.1 seconds, the canopy did not complete pressurization before impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Excessive delay for stowed deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Brandon intentionally planned an approximately 4-second delay from a bridge (approximately 486 feet), slider-down with a stowed BOC deployment. The domain reference establishes that delays under 2 seconds should generally use hand-held deployment because the time window to locate and deploy the PC is very limited and altitude margin is small. A 4-second delay from a span of this height, while slider-down, consumed the vast majority of available altitude before any deployment attempt, leaving zero margin for the deployment difficulty he encountered.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued past abort point
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper received a specific briefing that at the decision point near power lines, if speed was insufficient or any doubt existed, the correct action was to pull immediately. Outside observers noted he lost speed but decided to keep going anyway. This decision to press on past the abort point without adequate speed was the producing mechanism, resulting in insufficient altitude to deploy after clearing subsequent obstacles.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed brake release after correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After experiencing a 90° off-heading opening to the left, the jumper corrected heading with rear risers but took 'precious seconds' to release his brakes. This delayed transition from riser correction to brake flight cost critical altitude and forward distance, leaving him too low to clear the pool and reach the main landing area.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued to main LZ when too low
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After losing considerable altitude during the heading correction, the jumper decided to continue forward toward the main landing zone rather than landing in the available emergency landing areas to the left or right. The narrative states he was already low but 'decided to go forward for the main landing zone' and could not clear the pool with the strong water current, resulting in drowning.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Canopy entanglement during TARD
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During a TARD (Totally Awesome Rapid Deployment) exit, the jumper's leg or foot became caught in the canopy, causing him to fall forward into the canopy until impact. This is a technique/execution error during an unpacked deployment method that requires precise handling to avoid jumper/canopy entanglement. The domain reference explicitly notes that TARDs require the jumper to toss the canopy upward to avoid entanglement, and that any wrong move can cause entanglement.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed and altitude during flight corrections
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper made a drastic 90° turn to the right to avoid a ledge, then corrected 90° back to the left. These aggressive maneuvers bled speed and altitude, leaving him flying very low and slow with insufficient energy to clear the powerlines ahead. The turns resulted in a loss of speed-glide that he could not recover from, producing the fatal impact in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle stowage and rigging execution error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper failed to stow the excess bridle between the two break cord loops with a rubber band, and used a static cord loop that was too long for the site, creating excessive free bridle/cord that could entangle. This rigging execution error—leaving unstowed excess bridle in a static line configuration—directly enabled the bridle to wrap around his foot during exit, resulting in a total malfunction (PC in tow / bridle entanglement) and fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor exit technique with leg flexion
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the exit, the jumper flexed his legs excessively toward his body, intercepting the excess bridle with his right foot. This execution error in body position during a static line exit directly caused the bridle entanglement that prevented canopy deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient exit push
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper experienced a weaker push than usual on exit from a technically demanding exit point with a ledge requiring clearance (-20+6m ledge). The insufficient forward separation resulted in him clipping the ledge shortly after exit, leading to subterminal impact. The exit technique/execution was inadequate for the known terrain requirements, not the decision to jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Aggressive bank caused loss of control
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exiting, the jumper applied a very sharp bank to the right that was too aggressive, sending him into a steep, head-low, sideways attitude. Although he recovered from the upset, the altitude lost during the recovery left him too low to clear the ledge terrain on his chosen right-side line, resulting in impact with trees.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Aggressive proximity line selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly 'a very committing and aggressive turn into a drainage,' a proximity line that left insufficient margin for error. Based on the body location and known flight characteristics, the jumper selected a flight path requiring a high-commitment maneuver near terrain that produced impact in terminal freefall. The narrative describes the jumper flying with 'commitment and intensity' and recognizing that 'pushing further would be unsustainable,' indicating the chosen line was at or beyond the edge of sustainable risk.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown mechanism of PC failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper died from a PC in tow or PC-bridle entanglement resulting in closed-container or near-closed-container impact (canopy only slightly out of container). The narrative does not provide sufficient detail to determine whether the fatal outcome resulted from a decision error (e.g., incorrect rigging setup) or a performance error (e.g., weak PC toss, deployment technique failure). The possible factors list mentions both incorrect rigging setup and PC entanglement, but the investigation was incomplete and the specific mechanism cannot be determined from available information.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed to release PC cleanly
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper grabbed the PC by the handle but failed to deploy it immediately and cleanly. After losing his helmet, he reached for it with his hand while still holding the PC, allowing the bridle and PC to blow forward and wrap around his arm. This hesitation and continued grip on the PC handle during the distraction caused a bridle-under-arm entanglement that resulted in a PC in tow / total malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Terrain impact during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was performing wingsuit proximity flying and impacted terrain just before entry into a gully he intended to fly through, with nothing deployed. A witness who exited after him believes he miscalculated the entry point. The fatal outcome resulted from inadequate execution of the proximity line — specifically, failing to maintain sufficient separation from terrain during the approach to the gully, resulting in impact in terminal freefall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Continued past abort criterion
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The group pre-established a reference point at approximately 370m vertical/600m horizontal where, if not reached with good horizontal speed, the jumper would abort to the river landing at 900m. Gonsholt arrived below this reference point yet chose to continue toward the bottom valley landing. This decision committed him to a flight path requiring sustained high glide ratio he could not maintain, resulting in 48 seconds of flight at 3-15m AGL in a continuous no-pull zone with decaying airspeed, ending in ground impact on line stretch after an emergency pull at 70 seconds.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unknown cause of terminal impact
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper died from impact in terminal freefall, meaning the parachute was never deployed. The narrative suggests altitude awareness may have been a factor, and bad weather with clouds, rain, and low visibility may have contributed. However, the narrative provides insufficient detail to determine whether this was a decision error (e.g., choosing to jump in conditions that prevented altitude awareness) or a performance/skill-based error (e.g., failing to deploy due to spatial disorientation or lost altitude awareness during freefall). The first jumper's account of seeing the canopy impacting the wall appears inconsistent with the stated cause of death being terminal freefall impact, adding ambiguity to the sequence of events.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew unknown technical proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Jeremiah decided to fly through a technical notch on his first visit to the object, without laser/GPS data, without prior flights from this exit, and despite being told his approach to assessment was inadequate and that safer alternatives existed. He acknowledged uncertainty about whether his suit could clear the terrain. The decision to attempt this line — which required precise knowledge of terrain clearance and subterminal turning performance he had no data for — was the producing mechanism for his impact with terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed and glide
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew his wingsuit very slowly, as observed by two witnesses who were immediately concerned upon seeing his flight. This slow flight degraded his glide performance and available lift, causing him to lose altitude and fail to clear the plateau (which required a glide ratio over 3:1). The resulting low altitude left him in a no-pull zone or with insufficient altitude for canopy flight, leading to a fatal emergency pull with a downwind opening and high-speed terrain impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate line selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a flight line over plateau La Marchande that required a glide ratio exceeding 3:1 and featured a long no-pull zone, despite conditions that degraded performance: high altitude (reducing air density and wingsuit performance), morning thermals producing variable conditions, limited mountain experience, and only approximately 20 jumps on his Corvid wingsuit. This line selection left zero margin for the below-average flight performance he demonstrated, and the long NPZ meant that once performance was insufficient, there was no survivable deployment option.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed/glide near terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was flying a wingsuit proximity line near pinnacles at or near maximum glide speed in thermic conditions. He failed to maintain adequate speed and separation to clear the terrain feature (pinnacle), resulting in either a stall, clipping a tree, or being unable to clear the obstacle. The tourist video shows him at the second-to-last pinnacle in what appeared to be slow gliding flight before a steep dive or tumble, consistent with flying near stall speed at max glide with insufficient energy reserves to clear terrain. He impacted without deploying his parachute.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity flight at inadequate margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly a proximity line over a pinnacle at near-maximum glide ratio, which the domain reference identifies as flying at or below MID speed with zero energy reserves and no margin for error. Flying at max glide near terrain features in thermic conditions (variable lift/sink and possible rotors near pinnacles) left no margin to convert speed to altitude if conditions were slightly worse than expected. Multiple peers noted his aggressive proximity flying style was 'not sustainable,' and the specific decision to attempt clearing the pinnacle at near-max-glide in variable thermic conditions was clearly inadequate for the situation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient exit separation from waterfall
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited with insufficient forward separation from the waterfall. After a 100-degree off-heading opening to the left, the canopy edge contacted the waterfall despite an attempted correction. The lack of horizontal distance from the waterfall meant that even a moderate off-heading placed the canopy within reach of the water flow, which then collapsed and entrained the canopy, resulting in a fatal subterminal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed his exit with a strong over-rotation that led to immediate head-low instability and complete loss of control. Despite having a practiced exit technique (arms swing up, down, then two-foot static launch), the execution on this jump produced an unrecoverable unstable attitude. The jumper tumbled uncontrolled down the rock face in terminal freefall without deploying his parachute, resulting in fatal impact approximately 5-6 seconds after exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: GoPro footage shows Fabrizio had a bad exit that caused him to go very head low. Although he immediately attempted to correct the dive, the terrain under the exit did not provide sufficient altitude for recovery. The execution error on exit — going head low in a wingsuit — was the producing mechanism for the fatal subterminal impact, as it consumed the available altitude margin before flight could be established.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Premature progression to slider-up cliff
L1_unsafe_acts.description: With only ~25 BASE jumps, limited to antenna and slider-down cliff experience, guided by an inexperienced mentor, Yaroslav attempted his first slider-up cliff jump at a site with only 250-270m vertical and 30-50m of talus below. This was a clearly inappropriate decision: the domain reference establishes that slider-up cliffs require sub-terminal tracking competence, extensive object avoidance practice at forgiving objects, and careful progression. The jumper himself acknowledged the approach was 'roulette,' and experienced jumpers explicitly warned him. The combination of first slider-up jump, insufficient training, sub-terminal cliff with limited separation margin, and inadequate mentorship made this decision identifiably and substantially wrong. The site's limited vertical relief and talus meant that any off-heading opening—a known and expected occurrence, especially with slider-up heading degradation at sub-terminal speeds—left virtually no margin for correction, directly producing the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate PC deployment technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper 'gently threw' the pilot chute in strong crosswind conditions from a 60-meter antenna. The weak/gentle PC toss failed to deploy the PC into clean airflow with sufficient force, allowing the crosswind to carry the PC across his body, resulting in the bridle wrapping around his body or camera mount. A forceful, deliberate throw into clean air to the side is the accepted technique to prevent PC/bridle entanglement, and the jumper failed to execute this.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited too head-down, which is a recognized wingsuit exit technique error. This caused him to lose altitude during recovery, left him pointed toward higher terrain on the left, and forced a corrective turn to the right that consumed additional altitude. The cascading altitude loss from the poor exit ultimately left him too low to clear terrain or deploy, resulting in high-speed ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped with skydiving gear from building
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump a 14-story building using a skydiving canopy (Sabre 2 150 sqft) in slider-up configuration. This is a fundamentally inappropriate equipment choice for a building jump: skydiving canopies are not designed for BASE applications, slider-up configuration from a building provides insufficient altitude for the slow opening sequence, and the canopy's flight characteristics (high aspect ratio, high forward speed, no deep brake setting) are unsuitable for the confined landing environment. The decision to use skydive gear for a BASE jump was the producing mechanism for the fatality, as the equipment could not function adequately for this application regardless of execution quality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attempted BASE jump without training or experience
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had only 147 skydives (below the accepted 200+ minimum prerequisite) and had completed only 1 prior BASE jump. He had no BASE-specific equipment, no apparent FJC or mentorship, and attempted a building jump — a highly technical object requiring experienced jumpers with refined canopy skills and understanding of urban wind effects. This represents a clear departure from accepted progression norms and a decision that was identifiably inadequate.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle routing/deployment error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper either failed to perform a final check of the static line routing (allowing the bridle to pass in front of his right arm) or incorrectly threw the pilot chute over rather than under his arm, resulting in the bridle becoming entangled around his arm. This execution error caused premature break cord rupture, body position disruption, and a compromised deployment that the jumper could not recover from at the available altitude.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped in known strong winds
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumpers knew there were strong southwest winds (~50km/h) at the primary exit point and relocated to a secondary exit that was in the lee of a mountain, mistaking the wind shadow for safe conditions. The decision to jump was clearly and specifically wrong: the paraglider/hang glider ramp nearby was closed due to strong winds, all experienced local jumpers they contacted had declined to jump due to unsuitable weather, and the strong winds were a known macro condition that would be encountered once leaving the wind shadow. This decision directly produced the fatal outcome when the jumper exited the lee-side shelter and encountered severe turbulence and rotor winds that dragged him into buildings and a highway.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Chose exit requiring mandatory clearance beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to exit from a point with considerable talus to clear into tight terrain, after observing the first jumper barely clear the same obstacle. The decision to jump from this exit point was clearly and specifically inappropriate given the observable evidence that clearance was marginal even for the preceding jumper, and the terrain offered no margin for a below-average start. This was the producing mechanism: the exit point selection committed the jumper to a flight path that required near-perfect performance with no tolerance for error, resulting in subterminal impact on the talus.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate speed management during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper reached reference points on level but with less speed than required. After the left corner, he deviated slightly left past an outcrop rather than flying straight to the ridge, then made a slight right turn to line up. By that point he was low and cornered with insufficient energy to clear the ridge. The flare attempt failed because the suit was underpowered at that speed. This represents a failure in execution of the chosen flight line — inadequate speed/energy management and suboptimal trajectory through the technical section — rather than a fundamentally wrong decision, given he had practiced this line on multiple prior jumps.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed off-heading correction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the canopy opened fully, it turned left and back toward the cliff, resulting in object strike. The jumper failed to execute a timely heading correction (via rear risers or brakes) to prevent flying back into the cliff. With only 10 BASE jumps, the jumper likely lacked the practiced muscle memory for instant off-heading corrections that could have prevented the object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Jumped site beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper had only 3 wingsuit BASE jumps and was known to have problems with his starts (wingsuit exits). He was specifically warned that this site was not appropriate for fixing or learning proper wingsuit exits, yet he chose to jump it anyway. With 3 WS BASE jumps and acknowledged start problems, the decision to jump a site that did not tolerate poor starts was a clearly inappropriate decision given the information available to him.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor wingsuit exit and delayed deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper most likely had a poor exit, lost altitude trying to get the wingsuit flying, and by the time he decided to deploy it was too late. The canopy was found out but had insufficient altitude to inflate, resulting in death from low pull. The execution failure in the exit and the delayed deployment decision during the emergency were the direct producing mechanisms of the fatality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Delayed deployment initiation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Bryce initiated his opening sequence too late after proximity flying, leaving insufficient altitude to recover from execution problems. The narrative explicitly states he would be alive if he had disconnected from terrain and begun his pull sequence three seconds earlier. He had been warned by close friends about his 'bad habit of pulling low' and had already experienced a consequence (water landing after low pull from Altissimo), yet continued the practice. The decision to delay deployment initiation until an altitude with zero margin for error was the producing mechanism — the subsequent mispulls, which would have been survivable at adequate altitude, became fatal only because of this decision.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed pilot chute extraction
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Bryce failed to extract the pilot chute on his first and second attempts, and his third attempt produced only a weak pull. The deployment sequence took approximately 6 seconds across three attempts (00:48 to 00:54), with the PC finally out at 00:54 and impact at 00:55. He was not wearing gloves or extra layers, so the mispulls represent a technique/execution failure in BOC extraction during wingsuit flight rather than a gear-access issue. While the low pull altitude was the decision that made these mispulls fatal, the mispulls themselves were an independent execution failure that directly contributed to the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to track for object separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper took a 10-second delay from a slider-up cliff but performed no tracking whatsoever, and experienced 100-110 degrees of uncontrolled side rotation during freefall. Sub-terminal tracking is mandatory for slider-up cliffs to gain separation from the object. The complete absence of tracking and freefall control resulted in opening very close to the wall with insufficient separation, directly producing the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: No corrective action under canopy
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After opening 120-140 degrees off-heading from her exit push direction and very close to the wall, the jumper took no visible corrective action on the canopy during the 1.5 seconds before impact. While time was extremely limited, the absence of any heading correction attempt (rear risers or brakes) contributed to the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable wingsuit exit technique
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a very head-high exit with a fully exposed leg wing, resulting in a scorpion that drifted the jumper backwards toward the wall. This execution error in exit technique directly produced insufficient object separation, causing the jumper to clip a ledge at 5 seconds. The exit technique failure was the producing mechanism for the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Late deployment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was found with the parachute out and slider still up, indicating the pilot chute was extracted too late to allow sufficient altitude for the canopy to fully deploy. The cause of death was low pull, and the possible factors note 'Extracted Pilot Chute too late,' indicating a failure in execution timing rather than a decision to delay intentionally beyond plan.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failed exit/start execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted terrain between the first and second ledge at approximately 270m below the 2170m exit point, dying from subterminal impact near the exit. Given that he was flying a one-piece tracking suit (not a wingsuit) from a site requiring a technical start, the fatal outcome was produced by a failure in execution during the exit and initial flight phase — whether through a weak/offset push, failure to achieve a flying position quickly enough, or inability to build adequate speed and separation from terrain. The jumper had previously jumped this exit successfully in the same suit, indicating the decision to jump the line was within his demonstrated capability, but execution on this occasion was fatally insufficient.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unqualified PCA assistant
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper arranged for a non-BASE-jumper to perform the PCA. The PCA assistant dropped the PC early, which at this cliff is described as 'catastrophic.' Entrusting the PCA—a life-critical deployment method where the assistant's timing and technique directly determine canopy extraction—to an unqualified person was a decision that produced the fatal malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Undersized pilot chute selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper was advised by friends to use a 48-inch PC but chose to use a 42-inch PC instead. For a PCA deployment at a slider-down cliff with minimal altitude, the smaller PC provided less drag force, compounding the early-release error and contributing to the PC-in-tow / total malfunction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Selected object beyond capability
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper selected an exit point (Super Gorgeous) that by the witness's own account had 'very little to no margin for error' and transitioned 'directly to terrain flight immediately after exit,' where even the experienced witness could not 'outfly the terrain with any additional altitude beyond what was needed to fly at a minimum.' The jumper was not a terrain flyer, yet chose an object that required immediate terrain flying from the start arc. This was a decision that was clearly and specifically inappropriate given the jumper's skill set — the object demanded a specialized capability (terrain flying) that the jumper did not possess, and the zero-margin nature of the jump meant any shortfall in start arc performance would be fatal.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inadequate exit and flight performance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The witness observed that the jumper's forward speed was 'not quite as high as I would expect during the first four to five seconds,' he was slightly right shoulder low from the start, and was crabbing right to left rather than achieving clean forward flight. This suboptimal exit and flight execution — insufficient speed and imperfect body configuration during the critical start arc — meant the jumper failed to generate the glide performance needed to clear the terrain, resulting in impact approximately six seconds after exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Off-heading opening from gainer
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a gainer exit from a slider-up cliff and experienced a 180-degree off-heading opening to the left, resulting in immediate object strike against a slightly positive wall. The exit was described as looking 'solid and symmetrical,' suggesting the intended technique was correct but the execution of the deployment sequence (body position at deployment, PC deployment, or heading management during opening) was insufficient, producing the fatal 180-degree off-heading with inadequate separation from the wall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable exit causing insufficient separation
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Thomas performed a one-step exit that was slightly unstable, brought his arms back too soon with legs slightly bent, causing an over-rotation/scorpion. His correction into a flat tracking position rather than maintaining a steeper angle resulted in clipping the ledge with his lower legs by approximately 20cm, sending him into violent unstable rotation from which he did not recover or deploy his parachute before impacting the wall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After clipping the ledge and being sent into violent rotation, approximately 6 seconds passed before wall impact with nothing deployed. The narrative notes a panic pull might have saved his life, indicating that despite available time, Thomas failed to initiate deployment, likely due to disorientation or shock from the impact with the ledge.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Loss of speed and glide
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper lost speed and glide during wingsuit flight along the Cachaca line, resulting in terrain impact in terminal freefall. The narrative explicitly states loss of speed-glide as the factor, indicating a failure in wingsuit piloting execution — maintaining adequate airspeed and angle of attack — rather than a decision error in selecting the line. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, meaning the jumper never deployed a parachute, consistent with flight into a no-pull zone after losing performance.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committed proximity line with insufficient margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flying line that was deeper and lower than usual past terrain features, leaving no viable escape route. When he realized he was too low to clear the second ridge, his only option was a turn toward lower terrain, but this turn was itself a margin-consuming maneuver in close proximity. The narrative explicitly states he 'rarely jumped the same exit or flew the same line several times' and his lines were 'committed,' indicating a pattern of choosing novel, high-commitment flight paths. The specific line selection—flying deeper than usual, flattening out lower than usual past obstacles, with a ridge oriented 90° ahead—was a decision that eliminated outs and produced the fatal outcome independent of execution quality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Excessive delay slider-down
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper held the pilot chute for approximately 6 seconds on a slider-down deployment from a 1300ft cliff, despite the pre-jump plan calling for a ~1.5 second delay. A 6-second delay slider-down results in deployment at near-terminal airspeed with a slider-down configuration, which is explicitly contraindicated (the PC/delay chart shows slider-down is dangerous above 4 seconds). This produced the hard opening that likely rendered him unconscious or dazed, leaving him unable to correct a slow off-heading turn into the wall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Bridle routing error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited with the bridle routed between/around his leg, causing a bridle entanglement that prevented the static line deployment from achieving line stretch. The witness describes nearly making the same mistake himself - routing the bridle between the legs when stepping over the railing - indicating this was an execution error during exit preparation rather than a deliberate decision. The entanglement resulted in PC in tow / no line stretch and closed-container impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Terrain impact during proximity flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted a tree while flying in proximity along the ridge to the left of the exit point, prior to making a planned left turn toward the tree gates. Despite being described as a technically proficient flyer with nearly perfect starts, the jumper failed to maintain adequate separation from terrain/obstacles during the proximity portion of the flight. The cause of death was impact in terminal freefall, indicating no deployment was initiated, consistent with an unexpected collision with a tree during active wingsuit flight.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient exit push and unstable exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited with 'not the greatest push, maybe a bit to the side' and pulled immediately after exit from a slider-down cliff. The inadequate forward separation and possibly asymmetric exit produced insufficient object clearance, and the canopy opened with a 180° off-heading resulting in object strike. The jumper intended to exit and deploy successfully but failed in execution quality — the exit push and body position were insufficient to generate the separation needed to survive an off-heading opening.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate line selection
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Dylan chose a flight line far to the right over terrain that, according to the witness, would require thermals to be viable but was being flown in early morning calm conditions when no thermals were present. This line selection was the producing mechanism: without adequate thermal lift to support the chosen trajectory, the jumper lost glide performance over terrain and impacted in terminal freefall. The witness explicitly stated this line would be possible later with thermals but would be difficult in the early morning without them.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Proximity line beyond skill level
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a proximity flight line that was 'too close to the limit' despite not having developed adequate wingsuit/tracking suit flying skills. A close friend confirmed the jumper experienced significant rolling and pitching instability in the suit, that he 'wasn't very good at flying,' and that he had been warned on multiple occasions that he was pushing beyond his skill level. The decision to fly a proximity line requiring clean, precise flight with a suit he could not reliably control was a clearly inappropriate course of action that produced the fatal terrain impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable head-low exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited High Ultimate very head-low and transitioned onto his back, completing the first half of a front flip. This unstable exit resulted in an uncontrolled trajectory that caused him to impact a lower ledge subterminal. The jumper had experienced a very head-low exit from the same object the day prior, indicating a recurring execution problem with his exit technique at this specific site.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Committed to unsurvivable proximity line
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose a flight line that took him right into terrain below tree level with no escape route, rather than turning left to the known exit from the terrain. The narrative describes him turning right 'into what looked like flat terrain' after the houses, becoming boxed in below tree level with no way to turn left to fly out. Despite intimate knowledge of the terrain from 15-16 prior flights, the chosen line committed him to a path where even a maximum flare was insufficient to clear the trees, resulting in impact in terminal freefall. The decision to fly that specific line segment—rather than taking the known exit to the left—was the producing mechanism for the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable aerial exit causing off-heading
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper performed a 'tweener' (aerial maneuver) from a slider-down cliff jump and experienced a 140-degree off-heading opening with line twists. The aerial likely contributed to an unstable body position at deployment, producing the off-heading and line twist that sent him into the wall. The execution of the aerial and/or deployment was insufficient, as evidenced by the severe off-heading and line twist, resulting in object strike before any correction could be made.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Flew too close to terrain
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew a technical wingsuit line with multiple sharp turns extremely close to the ground over a glacier, in conditions where katabatic airflow was producing sinking air. The companion explicitly states the main reason for the accident was 'flying so close to the ground' and that they flew closer than on previous occasions. The combination of consecutive tight turns (which bleed speed and available lift) at minimal terrain clearance in sinking katabatic conditions left no margin for recovery, resulting in terrain impact at terminal velocity.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Insufficient terrain margin
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew a wingsuit proximity line too close to terrain, leaving insufficient margin to clear a hill. The companion's reconstruction indicates he realized too late he could not pass the terrain feature, attempted an escape left into a bowl, and impacted. Multiple experienced peers had repeatedly warned him about flying close to terrain despite his low experience level (80 BASE jumps, 3 seasons), indicating a pattern of accepting clearly inappropriate risk in proximity flying.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Unstable/head-low exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper impacted in terminal freefall very close to the exit point, indicating failure to achieve a flyable wingsuit configuration after launch. The narrative reports two plausible mechanisms: slipping at the exit or jumping too head-low. The jumper was known to have head-low exits ('He use to have head low exits'), and either a slip or an excessively head-low launch represents a technique/execution error that prevented the wingsuit from generating adequate lift to fly away from terrain. The cause of death—terminal freefall impact close to the exit—confirms the exit execution failure directly produced the fatal outcome.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Inappropriate aerial maneuver
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to perform a gainer (backflip exit) from a slider-up cliff object despite only ever jumping his wingsuit at this type of object, having no established competence in aerials from slick cliff jumps. The domain reference explicitly states aerials exponentially increase danger, require careful planning, instruction, supervision, and a forgiving object, and that extensive training is mandatory. The gainer caused over-rotation and instability that directly led to poor body position, inadequate object separation, and a 180-degree off-heading opening facing the wall, producing the fatal sequence of object strikes.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Changed exit point without adequate assessment
L1_unsafe_acts.description: On arrival, the jumper and partner chose a new exit point 20 meters higher than the planned one, offset to the right of the flight axis and the deepest part of the couloir. The new exit was in a shaded couloir where landscape details—including a ledge on the flight trajectory—were imperceptible from the exit point. The decision to use this inadequately assessed exit point produced a flight plan that 'did not correspond to reality,' placing the jumper on a collision course with hidden terrain.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Overcorrection during sub-terminal wingsuit flight
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Upon recognizing the hidden ledge, the jumper initiated a left input to avoid it. At sub-terminal speed the input had little immediate effect, so he reinforced it. When airspeed increased seconds later, the cumulative input became excessive, causing an uncontrollable deviation to the left and impact with rock. This was an execution error—the correct action was identified but the magnitude of control input was poorly calibrated to the changing airspeed, directly producing the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor exit execution
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited from an uncomfortable position with his upper body sideways to the left of the jump direction, resulting in a rotation to the right. His foot caught on the rail, causing a head-low over-rotation. This poor exit execution directly produced the unstable body position that led to the 130-degree off-heading opening and subsequent fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to correct off-heading
